title: Etsy Fees vs Shopify: A Side-by-Side Cost Breakdown for Handmade Sellers
description: Real comparison of Etsy and Shopify fees for a $35 handmade product: listing, transaction, payment, offsite ads, shipping label and Shopify subscription. Includes breakeven volume analysis.
excerpt: Etsy and Shopify look like the same job — sell handmade goods — but their fee structures diverge at
body: Etsy and Shopify look like the same job — sell handmade goods — but their fee structures diverge at every line. Here is the real cost on a $35 product, plus the breakeven volume where each wins. Fee stack on a $35 product Fee Etsy Shopify Basic + Stripe Subscription $0 $39/month Listing / product page $0.20 $0 Transaction fee 6.5% = $2.28 0% Payment processing 3% + $0.25 = $1.30 2.9% + $0.30 = $1.32 Platform + payment total $3.78 $1.32 Offsite ads (15% on attributed sales) $5.25 (if attributed) $0 Total with offsite ads $9.03 $1.32 Shipping label discount 10–30% off USPS Variable Etsy is cheaper per order for low-volume sellers, but offsite ads and the $0.20 listing can flip the math fast. Listing fees in detail Every Etsy listing costs $0.20. Listings auto-renew every 4 months, or when sold. 100 active listings, 4 months no sales: $20 in renewal fees 100 active listings, all sold in 4 months: $0 (renewed on sale) For sellers with high SKU counts and slow turnover, listings add up. description: Etsy 和 Shopify 在 $35 手作产品上的真实费用对比：上架费、成交费、支付费、站外广告、运费标签、Shopify 月费。含盈亏平衡点分析。
excerpt: Etsy 和 Shopify 看起来干同一件事——卖手作——但费用结构每个条目都不一样。下面是 $35 产品的真实成本，以及双方各占上风的销量临界点。 $35 产品费用堆栈 费用 Etsy Shopi
body: Etsy 和 Shopify 看起来干同一件事——卖手作——但费用结构每个条目都不一样。下面是 $35 产品的真实成本，以及双方各占上风的销量临界点。 $35 产品费用堆栈 费用 Etsy Shopify Basic + Stripe 月费 $0 $39/月 上架 / 商品页 $0.20 $0 成交费 6.5% = $2.28 0% 支付处理 3% + $0.25 = $1.30 2.9% + $0.30 = $1.32 平台 + 支付合计 $3.78 $1.32 站外广告（归因单 15%） $5.25（被归因时） $0 含站外广告合计 $9.03 $1.32 运费标签折扣 USPS 9 折–7 折 视情况 低销量 Etsy 单价更低，但站外广告和 $0.20 上架会把算账翻过来。 上架费细节 每个 Etsy 上架 $0.20。4 个月或售出时自动续。 100 个在线 listing，4 个月没卖出：续期费 $20 100 个在线 listing，4 个月全卖出：$0（卖出即续） SKU 多、动销慢的卖家，光上架就吃钱。1,000 SKU 的店每 4 个月花 $200 才能保持可见。 成交 + 支付费算账 订单金额 Etsy（6.5% + 3% + $0.25） Shopify Basic + Stripe（2.9% + $0.30） $10 $1.43 $0.59 $20 $2.23 $0.88 $35 $3.78 $1.32 $50 $5.00 $1.75 $100 $8.75 $3.20 每个价位 Etsy 抽的"平台 + 支付"都差不多是 Shopify 的 3 倍。 站外广告 Etsy 在平台外投广告（Google、Facebook、Pinterest）。年营收 $10K 以下，被归因的订单收 15%。$10K 以上降到 12%。 $10K 以下不能退出。这个区间里站外广告能把 Etsy 从"便宜"变成"贵很多"。 $35 产品 30% 归因率，每归因单加 $5.25。月 100 单、30% 归因 = $157.50/月，已经是 Shopify 月费的 4 倍。 运费标签折扣 Etsy 提供 USPS、FedEx、Canada Post 标签，9 折–7 折。$5 USPS Priority 标签，平台付 $3.50–$4.50。 Shopify Shipping 提供 USPS、UPS、DHL 等同价位。标签成本两边差不多，差别在平台费上加多少。 盈亏平衡：Etsy vs Shopify $35 产品、不算站外广告： 平衡点：3.78 = 39/n + 1.32，n = 39 / 2.46 ≈ 16 单/月。 月单 16 以下 Etsy 纯费用更便宜。超过 Shopify 占优。 加上站外广告后： 平衡点：9.03 = 39/n + 1.32，n

title: FX & Payout Comparator — PayPal, Wise, Airwallex
description: Compare what you actually receive across PayPal, Wise and Airwallex when converting USD, EUR, GBP or JPY into CNY, USD or EUR.
excerpt: Cross-border sellers pay 3–4 different fees when money moves from a customer''s card to a mainland C
body: Cross-border sellers pay 3–4 different fees when money moves from a customer''s card to a mainland China bank account: payment processing fee, FX margin, intermediary bank fee, withdrawal fee. Wise and Airwallex undercut PayPal specifically on FX margin, which is often the single largest leak. How this calculator works Amount — the gross amount you''re converting. From currency — your sales currency (USD most common). To currency — typically CNY for Chinese entities, USD for US-based. The tool returns: FX margin baked into each provider''s displayed rate. Withdraw fee (if any). Net receive amount — what hits your bank. Defaults assume mid-market reference rates as of writing; your actual rate depends on the day. The provider with the smallest gap between its fee column and the sum of others is the winner. Sample receive amounts (USD → CNY, $10,000) Provider FX margin Effective rate Receive CNY Loss vs mid-market Mid-market (reference) 0% 7.25 ¥72,500 ¥0 PayPal 3.5% 6.996 ¥69,963 ¥2,537 Wise 0.41% 7.220 ¥72,203 ¥297 Airwallex 0.35% 7.225 ¥72,246 ¥254 On a single $10k transfer, the gap between PayPal and Airwallex is $2,283 in your favor. title: GitHub 上 5 个开源 AI 副业剧本:2026 年「AI 赚钱手册」栈的元解读
description: 一个国人 operator 整理的 5 个开源 AI 副业剧本。覆盖:AI 赚钱手册(AI 工具、SaaS、自媒体、API 聚合、海外项目)、程序员副业合集、AI 内容流水线、AI 图像服务、AI 短剧制作。这条不是单个剧本——是对 2026 年 AI 副业剧本「反复出现的形状」的元解读。每个剧本结尾都是:工作流图、工具清单、一条陡得不合理的「营收曲线」。
excerpt: 一个国人 operator 在 GitHub 上整理了 5 个开源 AI 副业剧本。合集覆盖 AI 工具、SaaS、自媒体、API 聚合、海外项目、AI 内容制作、AI 图像服务、AI 短剧制作。案例
body: 一个国人 operator 在 GitHub 上整理了 5 个开源 AI 副业剧本。合集覆盖 AI 工具、SaaS、自媒体、API 聚合、海外项目、AI 内容制作、AI 图像服务、AI 短剧制作。案例不是单个剧本——是对 2026 年 AI 副业剧本「反复出现的形状」的元解读。每个剧本出货:一张工作流图、一个工具清单、一条陡得不合理的「营收曲线」。5 个里 4 个缺单位经济部分。元教训:剧本出货上行,跳过缺口,大多数读者在缺口里亏钱。下面是反复出现的形状、单位经济缺口、以及案例没展开的四点。 5 个剧本反复出现的形状 形状在 5 个剧本里一致。重点不是剧本写得不对——是剧本写得不全。剧本出货上行,跳过缺口。缺口就是单位经济风险待的地方。 单位经济缺口 缺口是结构性的。剧本假设无摩擦的分发渠道;现实是分发是瓶颈。剧本假设工具栈免费或近免费;现实是工具栈每月复利成本 200–2000 美金(规模化下)。 为什么剧本对头部 5–10% 有效、对中位数不奏效 剧本对有以下条件的 operator 有效: 一个分发渠道(现有受众、付费流量或内容引擎) 愿意迭代 prompt 设计与质量控制 财务跑道撑得住 3–6 月负现金流 对这些 operator,剧本是一个起始模板,他们调整并执行。对中位数读者——以上都没有——剧本是一个菜谱,产出几周活动,然后放弃。 剧本是写给 operator 的,不是写给读者的。按剧本照搬的读者在缺口里亏钱。把剧本当起始模板、补齐单位经济缺口的读者,有机会吃上行。 案例没说的事 四点。 作者营收。 剧本没给作者自己的营收,只整理第三方成功故事。作者自己的结果是核心证据;没有它,剧本是理论的。 中位结果。 没报中位结果,只报分布顶部。1–5% 的「成功率」合理但没说。 失败模式。 失败的剧本没被收录,只收录跑通的。生存偏差放大了暗示的成功率。 剧本对头部 5–10% operator 有效、对中位数读者无效这个结构性论点,案例没做。 案例把合集框定为正面资源;缺口是那个没说出的部分。 结论 案例不是「GitHub 上 5 个 AI 副业剧本」。案例是:2026 年 AI 副业剧本生态出货上行,跳过单位经济缺口。剧本对头部 5–10% 的 operator 有效(他们本来就有分发);对中位数读者不奏效。缺口是大多数读者亏钱的地方。 看这些剧本的多数读者,带走就一句:把剧本当起始模板看,不是菜谱。出货工作流之前,先补齐单位经济缺口。上行对有分发的 operator 是真的;对其他人是理论的。

title: Global Payment Methods by Region: The Complete 2026 Map
description: A continent-by-continent guide to wallets, internet payments, mobile payments, cards, prepaid cards, and cross-border payment services in 80+ countries.
excerpt: Digital payments vary dramatically by region. East Asia runs on mobile wallets, Europe on cards and
body: Digital payments vary dramatically by region. East Asia runs on mobile wallets, Europe on cards and instant bank transfers, Latin America on a hybrid of cards and cash vouchers, Africa on mobile money. Here is the country-by-country map. Asia: the most diverse payment landscape East Asia China has the world's highest mobile wallet penetration. Alipay and WeChat Pay cover 90% of urban consumers and dominate in-person QR-code payments. Supporting wallets: JDB Pay, Best Pay, Baidu Wallet, UnionPay Wallet. Internet payment platforms: Alipay Online, TenPay, UnionPay Online, 99Bill, ChinaPnr. Mobile apps from every major bank and payment institution enable QR scanning and remote transfers. Card networks: UnionPay (dominant domestically), Visa, Mastercard, JCB. Prepaid: carrier cards from China Unicom/Telecom/Mobile, plus retail gift cards. Cross-border: Alipay International, WeChat Pay International, UnionPay International, PingPong, Airwallex, LianLian Pay. Japan combines mature card networks with transit-flavored prepaid cards. Wallets: PayPay, LINE Pay, Rakuten Pay, au WALLET. Internet payment: JCB Net Payment, Rakuten Online Payment, GMO Payment Gateway. Cards: JCB, Visa, Mastercard,

title: Gojiberry AI: 一个 AI 销售员, 11 个月从 0 到 350 万美金年化, 把自己当第一个客户
description: Y Combinator 背书的 B2B AI 销售 agent, 11 个月做到 350 万美金年化。深度拆解: 单元经济、自我销售飞轮, 以及公开数据没说的三个数字。
excerpt: Gojiberry AI 是 B2B AI 销售 agent: 输入一个 URL, 系统找高意向潜客, 跑多渠道外联, 替客户约会议。创始人公开口径 11 个月做到 350 万美金年化营收, 2000
body: Gojiberry AI 是 B2B AI 销售 agent: 输入一个 URL, 系统找高意向潜客, 跑多渠道外联, 替客户约会议。创始人公开口径 11 个月做到 350 万美金年化营收, 2000+ B2B 销售团队在用, Y Combinator 背书。这是创始人的第二个 SaaS, 第一个在 50 万欧元年化时卖掉了。这个产品不是辅助销售, 是直接当一个销售。 产品到底在做什么 客户视角的完整流程: 买家是 B2B 销售团队, 原本要花 4000-6000 美金/月雇初级 SDR 来干这些活。产品替换的不是 Closer, 是 Closer 前面那一整段漏斗。 350 万 ARR 后面的算账 这就是隐含的 blended ARPU。在企业级 SaaS 里这是一个低 ARPU, 所以真正重要的单位是团队数, 不是单价。145 USD/月, 价格更接近"Stripe Atlas 加几个集成"的层级, 不是 Salesforce seat。靠量, 不靠抬价。 创始人的第一个 SaaS 50 万欧元年化时卖掉。第二个 11 个月跑到了 7 倍。这跟一个更广的观察一致:第二个产品跑得更快, 因为创始人已经有分发、客户开发直觉、定价手感。 为什么"自我销售"飞轮是关键 Gojiberry 被引用最多的是"产品自己卖自己"。两件具体的事: 产品是它自己的第一个客户。 AI agent 替 Gojiberry 找潜客、写外联序列、约 demo。替付费客户跑漏斗的系统, 就是替自己跑漏斗的系统。Gojiberry 没有独立的 marketing ops 流程。 单 URL 上手。 买家不配序列, 不写 prompt, 不建潜客名单。粘贴一个网站, agent 接手一切。从注册到约到会议, 时间是小时, 不是周。 这两件事干的是同一件事: 把"感兴趣"和"看到它真的在跑"之间的距离压到最短。当天午饭前就约到会议的买家, 跟 onboarding 了两周的买家, 是完全不同的买家。 这也是为什么这个模型作为 wrapper 很难抄。Wrapper 跑的是客户的配置。自我销售的产品是配置本身在生产里跑。两种架构分化很快。 价格锚定"人头", 不锚定 seat 结构性的定价洞察: 锚定 买家心智 结果 99 USD/月"AI 销售工具" 跟其他 AI 工具比 买家在功能表上挑 4000 USD/月 SDR 等价 跟招一个人比 买家在看约到的会是不是真的 Gojiberry 锚的是第二种。客户付的不是"AI 功能", 是"半个 SDR 的等价物"。在 145 USD/月这个隐含 ARPU 下, 买家心智更接近"一个帮我做前期外联的实习生", 不是"我在评估一个工具"。心智转移是账能算下来的关键。 通用打法 近期 B2B AI 工具里反复出现的模式: 选一个结果是日历事件的工作流

title: Gojiberry AI: An AI Sales Agent That Reached 3.5M USD ARR in 11 Months by Selling Itself
description: A B2B AI sales agent, Y Combinator-backed, that scaled from 0 to 3.5M USD annualized in 11 months. Cold-eyed read of the unit economics, the self-sell loop, and three numbers the public story quietly skips.
excerpt: Gojiberry AI is a B2B AI sales agent: input a URL, the system finds high-intent prospects, runs mult
body: Gojiberry AI is a B2B AI sales agent: input a URL, the system finds high-intent prospects, runs multi-channel outreach, and books meetings on the customer calendar. The founder publicly states 3.5M USD annualized revenue 11 months after launch, 2,000+ B2B sales teams using the product, Y Combinator-backed. This is the founder's second SaaS — the first sold at 500K EUR annualized. The product does not assist a salesperson. It acts as one. What the product actually does The workflow the customer sees: The buyer is a B2B sales team that would otherwise pay a junior SDR 4,000-6,000 USD/month to do this manually. The product does not replace the closer — it replaces the part of the funnel before the closer. The arithmetic behind 3.5M ARR That is the implied blended ARPU. It is a low ARPU relative to enterprise SaaS, which is why the unit that matters is team count, not price per team. At 145 USD/month, the product is priced closer to a "Stripe Atlas + a couple of integrations" tier than to a Salesforce seat. Volume is the play, not ARPU expansion. The founder's first SaaS sold at 500K EUR annualized. The second one is running roughly 7x the first in 11 months. The pattern is consistent

title: Google Ads ROAS 公式：真实的盈亏平衡计算
description: 怎么算 Google Ads ROAS、盈亏平衡 ROAS、基于实际利润率的目标 ROAS。含公式、计算器、常见 ROAS 错误。
excerpt: ROAS 是电商里引用最多的指标。多数卖家算错 — 庆祝亏损的广告活动。 ROAS 到底测什么 ROAS 4× 意味着每 $1 广告支出返回 $4 收入。这是毛数。完全没告诉你有没有赚钱。 盈亏平衡公
body: ROAS 是电商里引用最多的指标。多数卖家算错 — 庆祝亏损的广告活动。 ROAS 到底测什么 ROAS 4× 意味着每 $1 广告支出返回 $4 收入。这是毛数。完全没告诉你有没有赚钱。 盈亏平衡公式 其中 净利率 = 扣完全部成本（COGS、物流、平台费、退款）后的利润 ÷ 收入。 25% 净利率店铺：1 ÷ 0.25 = 4× 盈亏平衡 ROAS。 4× 以上广告支出就回本。4× 以下就是亏。 算例 店铺单品经济： 售价：$50 成本：$12 物流：$4 支付+平台费：$3（6%） 单件净利：$31（62% 利润率） 盈亏平衡 ROAS = 1 ÷ 0.62 = 1.61× 如果店铺达到 3× ROAS，归因到广告的单件利润： 3× ROAS 看起来很美，但广告单实际利润率 28.7%，不是 62%。广告花的钱是真金白银。 含利润缓冲的目标 ROAS 别只瞄盈亏平衡。加上其他成本的缓冲： 场景 25% 净利率店铺的目标 ROAS 盈亏平衡 4× 25% 利润缓冲 5.33× 50% 利润缓冲 8× 100% 利润缓冲（利润翻倍） 16× 多数店铺起步目标 2× 盈亏平衡 ROAS。连续 2 周低于盈亏平衡的广告系列直接砍。 常见 ROAS 错误 毛净混淆 50% 毛利率的产品（成本 = 售价的 50%）扣完费用、物流、退款后可能只有 20% 净利率。公式里用 50%，算出 2× 盈亏平衡，实际需要 5×。 忽略退货和拒付 15% 退货率的服装店要在成本上加 10%。如果"ROAS"不算退货，等于自欺欺人。 不扣支付处理费 $100 收入不是你的 $100。Stripe 拿 $3.20。算进公式。 辅助转化算错 Google 的"转化价值"包含浏览转化和跨设备。手机上看广告、桌面 Google 搜索后才下单的客户也被计入。这会让 ROAS 虚高 10–30%。 LTV 盲区 盈亏平衡 ROAS 的首单可以接受，如果 LTV 是首单 3 倍。但 LTV 和 AOV 一样的话，首单必须盈利。 报表时间窗口 24 小时的 ROAS 和 30 天 ROAS 看起来差很多。多数平台可自选。冷流量广告系列用 7 天或更长。 行业 ROAS 基准（美国电商 2026） 品类 中位数 ROAS 前 25% 服装 2.8× 5× 电子 3.5× 6× 家居 3.2× 5.5× 美妆 4× 7× 保健品 3.8× 6.5× 宠物 4.5× 7.5× 数字产品 6× 12× 这些都是行业聚合数。你店铺的盈亏平衡是决策时唯一重要的数字。 不涨价怎么提升 ROAS 质量得分 — 更好的广告、更相关的落地页。每 1 分质量得分通常降 CPC 10–15%。 人群分层 — 搜索和展示分开。搜索 ROAS 通常是展示 ROAS 的 3–5 倍。 否定关键词 — 每周加一次。多数浪费花费

title: Google Ads ROAS Formula: The Real Break-even Calculation
description: How to calculate Google Ads ROAS, break-even ROAS, and target ROAS based on your actual margin. Includes formula, calculator, and common ROAS mistakes.
excerpt: ROAS is the most quoted metric in ecommerce. Most sellers don't calculate it correctly — and end up
body: ROAS is the most quoted metric in ecommerce. Most sellers don't calculate it correctly — and end up celebrating unprofitable campaigns. What ROAS actually measures A ROAS of 4× means every $1 in ad spend returned $4 in revenue. That's the gross number. It tells you nothing about whether you made money. The break-even formula Where net margin = profit after all costs (COGS, shipping, fees, refunds) divided by revenue. For a 25% net margin store: 1 ÷ 0.25 = 4× break-even ROAS. Anything above 4× is profit on ad spend alone. Anything below is loss. Worked example A store with these unit economics: Sale price: $50 Cost: $12 Shipping: $4 Payment + platform fees: $3 (6%) Net profit per sale: $31 (62% margin) Break-even ROAS = 1 ÷ 0.62 = 1.61× If the store achieves 3× ROAS, profit per sale attributed to ads: 3× ROAS looks great but the actual margin on ad-driven sales is 28.7%, not 62%. The ad cost is real money you spent. Target ROAS with profit buffer Don't aim for break-even. Add a buffer for other costs: Scenario Target ROAS at 25% net margin Break-even 4× 25% profit buffer 5.33× 50% profit buffer 8× 100% profit buffer (double profit) 16× For most stores, target 2× break-even ROAS as a

title: Google Sheet + 自动邮件提醒卖给本地暖通维修老板 12 美金/月:2026 年无聊的 AI 生意不在 AI 工具里
description: 一个 12 美金/月的 Google Sheet + 自动邮件「客户跟进」产品,卖给本地蓝领服务业(HVAC)。怎么跑:一张 Google Sheet 记录所有安装,到点自动邮件提醒老板(不是客户)——三个月要换滤芯、保修快到期、半年保养要做。这条不是看 24 美金/月,是看可触达的市场——本地蓝领服务业老板(水电、清洁、园艺、汽车美容),他们的客户跟进几乎全靠脑子记。
excerpt: 一条知识星球帖子拆的是一名单人 operator,他给本地一家暖通维修(HVAC)店老板做了一个 Google Sheet + 自动邮件「客户跟进」产品。Sheet 记录这家店做过的所有安装,到点就自
body: 一条知识星球帖子拆的是一名单人 operator,他给本地一家暖通维修(HVAC)店老板做了一个 Google Sheet + 自动邮件「客户跟进」产品。Sheet 记录这家店做过的所有安装,到点就自动邮件提醒老板(不是客户):三个月要换滤芯了,提醒;保修快到期了,提醒;半年保养要做了,提醒。产品卖 12 美金/月。operator 又签了第二家店,产品现在跑在 24 美金/月。帖子把教训落点不是「24 美金」,而是可触达的市场——本地蓝领服务业老板,他们的客户跟进全靠脑子,因为这个丢掉了复购收入。 2026 年的 AI 工具市场已经饱和,AI 加持的本地服务市场没有。产品不是 AI——产品是「提醒」,卖给一个非技术买家,以面对面关系成交。AI 是自动邮件,栈里最便宜的那块。下面是单店单位经济、可触达的市场规模,以及案例没展开的四点。 产品本身 机械地讲,产品就是一张 Google Sheet 加一条定时自动邮件。没有模型、没有 agent、没有 app。杠杆不是 AI。杠杆是那个以前活在老板脑子里、但经常被忘掉的提醒。 单店单位经济 项目 数值 备注 单店价格 12 美金/月 案例数字 Onboarding operator 成本 2–3 小时 上门、导入历史、调时间表 月度维护 30 分钟 处理边界、看流失 单店净收入(规模化) 10 美金/月 扣掉 Stripe 手续费和时间分摊 流失(假设) 10%/月 本地 SaaS 典型值 回本周期 2–3 个月 净 10、onboarding 20–30 单店经济单薄。跨店经济才是看点。 可触达的市场 看单店金额没意义,要看 operator 能签多少店。 1% 转化(5,000 店),operator 跑出一个 60,000 美金/月的生意,毛利结构还是单店那种。数字不性感,但真。买家不是「AI 买家」——是「因为脑子记不住而丢掉复购」的本地服务业老板。 为什么 AI 工具 SaaS 死、这个活 三个原因。 产品以服务形式包装,不是软件。 一个服务业老板不会「装个 CRM」。但会签一个「你帮我搭的 Google Sheet」。人情包装做了 SaaS 着陆页做不到的事。 CAC 是本地的,不是数字的。 operator 走进店,搭好产品,当面签。CAC 是一个下午本地拜访的成本,不是 Google Ads 的成本。 买家非技术、价格无压力。 12 美金/月不需要采购批准。老板刷自己的卡,然后忘掉。99 美金/月的 AI SaaS 经常需要一张要跟会计报备的卡。 所以 2026 年,AI 工具市场饱和,AI 加持的本地服务市场没有。后者的竞争是 ServiceTitan、Housecall Pro、Jobber——但这些产品定价 200–500 美金/月,要几天 onboarding。一个 12 美金/月、靠一杯

title: GPT 套壳产品：8 种赚钱套路与 5 种烧钱陷阱
description: 8 种在财务上站得住脚的 GPT 套壳产品模式，5 种被 token 账单烧死的模式。
excerpt: 「GPT 套壳」两种意思。贬义：在底层模型之上什么都没加的薄产品。技术：任何坐在用户和基础模型之间的产品。2023-2025 大多数拿到融资的 AI 产品都是技术意义上的套壳。问题在哪些是贬义意义上的
body: 「GPT 套壳」两种意思。贬义：在底层模型之上什么都没加的薄产品。技术：任何坐在用户和基础模型之间的产品。2023-2025 大多数拿到融资的 AI 产品都是技术意义上的套壳。问题在哪些是贬义意义上的套壳。 8 种通常能赚钱的模式，5 种通常烧钱的模式。列表不穷尽，但后半部分的失败模式在每个例子里都共享。 8 种能成的模式 垂直工作流工具。 掌握一个行业里的具体岗位（律师的租约摘要、诊所的预授权草拟、货代的装箱单生成）。付费意愿高，因为替代方案是人来做。 Copilot 增强。 插件住在用户已经用的工具里——Slack bot、Notion 扩展、VS Code 插件、Shopify 应用。分发免费；CAC 接近零；切换成本是集成本身。 工作流自动化。 「带 AI 的 Zapier」，把用户的一句提示变成跨 API 的多步动作。按执行或按接入账号收费。 Prompt 市场 / IP 店。 高质量 prompt 和模板的精选库，卖给非技术用户。毛利 90%+；产品是策展和包装，不是模型。 一个垂直行业的 AI 客服。 替代某个具体行业里的一层人工客服（电商退货、SaaS 入门、牙科诊所登记）。买家的 ROI 具体可量。 面向电商的 AI 图像 / 视频。 商品摄影、列表图、短视频广告。买家替代方案是 200 美元/张摄影棚或 500 美元/条拍摄。 AI Agent 工作室 / 服务。 咨询公司，为中端市场客户搭建和运行 AI 工作流。产品是团队的时间和品味，不是工具。毛利像服务业（40-60%）。 内部带 AI 的高 ARPU 垂直 SaaS。 传统垂直 SaaS（诊所管理、美发预约、物业管理），AI 是其中一个功能。AI 是升级点；SaaS 是护城河。 5 种烧钱的模式 五种失败模式共享一个特征：在不可控的 token 账单上铺一个低的平价 ARPU。 20 美元/月无限制聊天。 任何聊天型套壳的重度用户每月烧掉 50-200 美元模型成本。20 美元平价座位在这些账号上是倒挂的。模式死于：一个重度用户上首页，公司要么全员重新定价，要么自己吃亏损。 通用的「与 PDF 对话」。 第一个用户能跑通。然后他们上传 200 页文档，token 账单超过用户终身价值。除非能按文档收费或对低价档硬卡，否则走不通。 作为消费应用的 AI 生图。 自 2023 年起，消费者为 AI 图像工具付费的意愿已经崩，底层模型在模型厂商自己的站点上免费或近免费。产品必须是工作流，不是图本身。 「X 行业的 AI」但不掌握 X。 给你不理解的行业做一个通用 AI 助手。分发难、模型 20% 的时候是错的、用户随时可以跑去模型厂商自己的产品。 个性化 AI 伴侣 / 朋友 / 教练。 用户兴趣高、付费意愿低、情感支持成本高、内容审核不断。能成的只有少数几个品牌化产品；不能泛化。 诊断

title: GPT Wrappers: 8 Patterns That Work, 5 That Burn Cash
description: Field guide to the 8 GPT-wrapper product patterns that hold up financially, and the 5 that die in the token bill.
excerpt: The phrase "GPT wrapper" means two very different things. The dismissive use: a thin product that ad
body: The phrase "GPT wrapper" means two very different things. The dismissive use: a thin product that adds nothing the underlying model does not already do. The technical use: any product that sits between the user and a foundation model. Most funded AI products in 2023-2025 are wrappers in the technical sense. The question is which of them are wrappers in the dismissive sense. Below are the 8 patterns that tend to make money, and the 5 that tend to burn cash. The failure mode in the second half is shared across every example. 8 patterns that work Vertical workflow tool. Owns a specific job in a specific industry (lease abstraction for lawyers, prior-auth drafting for clinics, packing-list generation for freight forwarders). High willingness to pay because the alternative is a human doing the work. Copilot augmentation. Lives inside the tool the user already uses — a Slack bot, a Notion extension, a VS Code plugin, a Shopify app. Distribution is free; CAC is near zero; switching cost is the integration. Workflow automation. A "Zapier with AI" that turns a user prompt into a multi-step action across APIs. Pricing is per execution or per connected account. Prompt marketplace / IP store.

title: Home-services AI workflow case: a quote-accept-follow-up loop, one operator, one niche — and three unit-economics gotchas the post skips
description: Cold-eyed case-study on selling a quote/acceptance/follow-up AI workflow to home-services SMBs. Pricing math, channel dynamics, and the three things the source quietly leaves out.
excerpt: A solo operator pitches home-services SMBs — cleaning, pest control, landscaping, handyman — on an A
body: A solo operator pitches home-services SMBs — cleaning, pest control, landscaping, handyman — on an AI workflow that does three jobs: standardized pricing, post-service acceptance, and follow-up. The pitch is blunt: inconsistent answers cost 20-30% of revenue. The deal shape is a one-time setup fee plus a monthly retainer. The post does not address three things that change the math: channel fragmentation across regions, staff override rate, and what happens when the bot mis-prices. It does not publish revenue, client count, or retention. The numbers below come from the operator's own framing. The arithmetic What compounds is the retainer. Five retainer clients give a 1,500-2,500 USD/month baseline that does not need new sales to hold. title: How a solo founder took Postiz past 145k USD MRR by integrating 30 platforms nobody else bothered with
description: A solo-built social-media scheduling tool reportedly hit 145k USD MRR by integrating 30+ publishing channels — Mastodon, Bluesky, Nostr, Warpcast, Telegram, Discord included. Cold read of why "channel count" beats "better UI" in tool-type products and how open-source turned into a paid-conversion funnel.
excerpt: A solo-built social-media scheduling tool called Postiz reportedly crossed 145k USD MRR. The product
body: A solo-built social-media scheduling tool called Postiz reportedly crossed 145k USD MRR. The product does not win on UI polish or feature depth. It wins on integration count — 30+ publishing channels, including the platforms competitors called "too small to bother with": Mastodon, Bluesky, Nostr, Warpcast, Telegram, Discord. The product is also open-source. Below is a cold read of why "channel count" is the moat and how open-source turns into a paid-conversion funnel. The number Quantity Value MRR (latest) 145k USD Implied ARR 1.74M USD Publishing channels 30+ Code license Open source Funding Bootstrapped (no round named) Headcount Solo founder Founder-attributed, not audited. The figure is a directional anchor for the strategy, not a financial statement. Why 30 channels is the moat The case names the move clearly. A 6-channel scheduling tool is in a feature-parity race with every other 6-channel scheduling tool. The buyer compares UI, price, and integrations. The tool has to win on at least one of those three to convert. A 30-channel scheduling tool is not in the same race. The buyer compares the tool to "hire a part-time social media manager to post on the other 24 platforms". Th

title: How to Prevent Payment Chargebacks (and Win Disputes When They Happen)
description: Practical chargeback prevention for cross-border ecommerce. Covers fraud screening, 3DS, evidence templates, and the dispute process on Stripe and PayPal.
excerpt: A chargeback isn't a refund. It's a $15–20 fee plus the product cost plus your time — even if you wi
body: A chargeback isn't a refund. It's a $15–20 fee plus the product cost plus your time — even if you win. Prevention is cheaper than defense. The two types of chargebacks Unauthorized (cardholder fraud) Someone used a stolen card to buy from you. The real cardholder notices and disputes. With 3DS in place, liability shifts to the issuer. Without 3DS, you eat the loss. Win rate with 3DS: 85% (issuer pays) Win rate without 3DS: 40% (you eat it) Merchant disputes (buyer remorse) Item not received (INR) — buyer claims product didn't arrive Significantly not as described (SNAD) — product differs from listing Service not provided — digital products, services These need evidence, not fraud filters. Win rate with strong evidence: 60–80%. Prevention checklist Before the sale [ ] Enable 3D Secure on all transactions. Stripe and PayPal both support it. Conversion drops 3–5%, but chargebacks drop 50%+. [ ] Use AVS (Address Verification System). Mismatched billing/shipping is a fraud flag. [ ] CVV required — never waive it. Most fraud doesn't have the CVV. [ ] Velocity checks — flag 3+ orders from same IP/card in 24h. [ ] Block high-risk countries if you don't ship there. List available in Stripe

title: Jordan Gal took an AI voice product from 0 to 1M USD ARR in 8 months with 6 people and a single 49 USD plan — the boring small-business voice funnel
description: A case profiles an AI voice product (Jordan Gal): 6-person team, 90 days to first version, 8 months to 1M USD ARR, single 49 USD/month plan throughout. The product replaces missed-call loss for local small businesses. Cold start via 1000 cold emails per day for 2 months. Pricing anchored against a human receptionist, not a software seat. The funnel math, the single-plan discipline, and the four gaps the case does not cover, below.
excerpt: An AI voice product targeting local small businesses that miss calls. 6-person team. 90 days to firs
body: An AI voice product targeting local small businesses that miss calls. 6-person team. 90 days to first version. 8 months to 1M USD ARR. Single 49 USD/month plan throughout the launch. Cold start via 1000 cold emails per day for 2 months. SEO + paid ads split 50/50 on acquisition. The interesting part is not 'AI makes voice calls' — it is the single-plan discipline and the cold-email CAC math. The funnel math, the single-plan discipline, and the four gaps the case does not cover, below. The unit economics at 1M USD ARR At 1M USD ARR, contribution margin (693K–898K USD) does not yet cover team cost (1.2M USD). The product is pre-profitable on team alone. To clear team cost, the operator needs roughly 2300–3000 paying customers, which the case implies is the next milestone. The cold-email CAC math The cold-email CAC math is the load-bearing assumption. At 1–3% reply rates in a high-intent voice vertical, the math works dramatically. If reply rates drop to 0.3–0.5% (which is closer to cold-email averages in 2026), the cohort economics compress. Why single-plan discipline Three reasons push single-plan: Decision cost. A small-business owner comparing 49 USD / 99 USD / 199 USD plans spend

title: Legora 定价数学:18 个月从 300 万美金 ARR 到 1 亿美金,因为它卖的是 AI 岗位,不是 AI 工具
description: 拆解瑞典法律 AI 公司 Legora 的营收轨迹(2024 年底 300 万、2025 年底 5000 万、2026 年 4 月 1 亿美金 ARR),以及为什么按人头定价、按软件订阅定价,会把垂直 AI 的单位经济学彻底拉开。
excerpt: 一个 operator 拆穿一个常见陷阱：开垂直 AI 产品，上来就按工具月费定价。Legora 是反面案例。案例列出三个营收里程碑——2024 年底 300 万美金 ARR、2025 年底 5000
body: 一个 operator 拆穿一个常见陷阱：开垂直 AI 产品，上来就按工具月费定价。Legora 是反面案例。案例列出三个营收里程碑——2024 年底 300 万美金 ARR、2025 年底 5000 万美金 ARR、2026 年 4 月 1 亿美金 ARR——同期 D 轮 6 亿美金、估值 56 亿美金。帖子的核心论点是：真正承重的是定价策略，不是模型表现。下面是拆解、定价数字含义，以及案例里没说的四个点。 案例摆出的数字 数量 值 2024 年底 ARR 300 万美金 2025 年底 ARR 5000 万美金 2026 年 4 月 ARR 1 亿美金 客户数 1000+ 家律所/法务团队 D 轮融资 6 亿美金,估值 56 亿美金 创始人/地区 Max Junestrand / 瑞典 命名交付物 合同尽调、合规监控、文书交付 数字由公司自报、未审计。案例用这些数字去论证定价策略才是关键的决策。 定价数学 横向 AI 写作工具的锚点是 ChatGPT 或 Claude Pro,20–50 美金/月，买家比的是下一个订阅、下一张账单的边际成本。垂直 AI 卖的不是「一个工具席位」，买家比的是： 一个 AI 初级律师 @ 50–150k 美金/年 一个人类初级律师 @ 150–200k 美金/年 一个能扛同样活的中级律师 @ 250–400k 美金/年 价格上限直接拉到 HR 的 FTE 预算池。销售单位从「seat」变成「FTE-equivalent」，数学差了大约两个数量级。 续费翻转 真正结构性后果在续费，不在获客。 定价模型 买家替代的是 买家流失的场景 横向 AI 写作工具(锚定 ChatGPT) 订阅清单上的一项 别家工具更好用,合同到续费时被价格比下去 垂直 AI 席位(锚定初级律师) 已经预算下来的人头 团队已经按 AI 重组,撤 AI 就要重发岗位、重启招聘 对横向工具，流失是价格比较的决策；对垂直 AI 席位，流失是组织架构的决策。组织架构决策比价格比较难回滚得多。成熟垂直 AI 厂商广宣的 60–80% 净留存，经济学就坐落在这上面。案例没指名，但定价策略已经暗示了它。 为什么大多数 operator 没踩到这条 帖子里说得直白：「卖工具一个月 99 美金，还是卖岗位一年 100k 美金，是 80% 留存工具类，还是 60–80% 留存 FTE 类的分界线。」 大多数人犯的错是上来就用软件月费的心智模型，因为所有横向 AI 工具都这么做，等被买家把价格压到 79 美金才奇怪，而自己本来是想收 200 美金。锚点定在软件月费，一开始就站错了参考系。 三个最容易踩坑的地方： 被替代的人头不够贵。 你替代一个 30k 美金/年的角色，价格天花板就在 15–20k,SaaS 的开销就要吃掉一半，单位经济学立不住。要选被替代的人头在 100k+

title: Legora’s pricing math: 3M USD to 100M USD ARR in 18 months by selling an AI seat, not an AI tool
description: Cold-eyed reading of Sweden-based Legora’s revenue trajectory (3M to 5,000M to 100M USD ARR in 18 months) and why pricing×headcount instead of pricing×seats changes the unit economics of a vertical AI tool.
excerpt: A operator walks through a common trap: starting a vertical AI product by pricing it like a tool. Sw
body: A operator walks through a common trap: starting a vertical AI product by pricing it like a tool. Sweden-based Legora is the counter-example. The case cites three revenue markers — 3M USD annualized at the end of 2024, 5,000M USD annualized by the end of 2025, and 100M USD annualized by April 2026 — alongside a 600M USD Series D at a 5.6B USD valuation. The post frames the trajectory as a pricing-strategy lesson rather than a model-performance story. Below is a cold read of the numbers, what the pricing choice implies, and what the case is silent on. What the case puts on the table Quantity Value End-2024 ARR 3M USD End-2025 ARR 5,000M USD April-2026 ARR 100M USD Customer count 1,000+ law firms and in-house legal teams Series D raise 600M USD at 5.6B USD valuation Founder / country Max Junestrand / Sweden Named deliverables Contract due diligence, regulatory compliance monitoring, document drafting The numbers are company-attributed. They are not audited. The case uses them as the reason to argue that pricing strategy is the load-bearing decision. The pricing math A horizontal AI writing tool is anchored at 20–50 USD/month (ChatGPT, Claude Pro, Jasper, copy.ai). The buyer compares

title: Local SMB AI Automation Freelance Case: One Operator, 5,000 USD/Month by Month 6 — and Why the AI Is the Least Interesting Part
description: Cold-eyed breakdown of a knowledge-planet case. Unit economics on local SMB clients, why offline sales beats Upwork bidding, and three failure modes the source quietly skips.
excerpt: A knowledge-planet post describes a solo operator who walks into local small businesses (dental clin
body: A knowledge-planet post describes a solo operator who walks into local small businesses (dental clinics, real estate agents, accounting firms), sells them AI-powered workflow integrations on Make.com plus a GPT layer, and reports 5,000 USD/month by month 6. The post is direct about numbers, direct about the stack, direct about the offline-vs-online gap. Three things it is not direct about: implementation friction, retainer churn, and the sales cycle length. Below is what the case actually adds up to, and the three gaps. What the case claims Quantity Value Setup fee per client 500 - 1,500 USD Monthly retainer 200 - 500 USD per client Clients in month 1 2 - 3 Month 3 revenue 2,000 USD/month Month 6 revenue (target) 5,000 USD/month Upwork AI freelance hourly rate 30 - 70 USD Cited Upwork category growth 109% YoY All figures are self-reported. The author's first name is omitted; the cited Upwork growth does not link to a primary report. The arithmetic The interesting part is the recurring share. The setup fee is the entry price; the retainer is what compounds. Five retainer clients give a 1,500-2,500 USD/month baseline that does not require new sales to maintain. The "5,000 USD by mont

title: Lovable 不到 200 人做到 5 亿美金 ARR:定价曲线里藏着什么
description: 瑞典 AI 应用生成器 Lovable 据披露在不到两年内做到 5 亿美金 ARR、不到 200 人、估值 66 亿美金。它不训自己的模型,把第三方大模型包成「描述需求 → 得到能跑的全栈应用」流程。拆单位经济学、定价档位实际意味什么,以及为什么它的目标客户不是开发者。
excerpt: Lovable,一支瑞典 AI 应用生成器,据披露在不到两年内做到 5 亿美金 ARR、不到 200 人、估值 66 亿美金。产品不训自己的模型,把第三方大模型包成「描述需求、得到一个能跑的全栈应用」
body: Lovable,一支瑞典 AI 应用生成器,据披露在不到两年内做到 5 亿美金 ARR、不到 200 人、估值 66 亿美金。产品不训自己的模型,把第三方大模型包成「描述需求、得到一个能跑的全栈应用」流程。下面拆单位经济学,以及定价档位实际意味什么。 数字 数量 值 年化营收(最新) 5 亿美金 员工数 <200 人均营收 200 万美金+/年 估值 66 亿美金 单轮融资 3.3 亿美金 ARR 最近一步增长 25%+ 每周新项目数 100 万 已上线项目月访问 6 亿 总用户数 800 万(过半用在能赚钱的活上) 数字为公司自报、未审计。用作方向性论据,不是财报。 定价档位 案例点了三档。形状比具体数字重要: 计费单位是项目复杂度与产出数量。这是「先出货,再按用量计费」——和 Vercel、和云 egress、和所有用量计费的基础设施产品同形。先免费,用户的第一个项目免费,价格随用户实际在做的量往上走。 这个形状是承重决策,不是具体价格点。具体价格点以后可以调。「免费起步,再按用量扩张」这个形状,是非技术买家能安心接受的——在做出他们想继续做的东西之前,他们不付钱。 为什么客户是「非技术」而不是「开发者」 开发者把 Lovable 对标到他们已有的栈:VS Code、Git、一个 LLM 订阅。月成本大约 0–20 美金。在开发者已经具备这套肌肉记忆的前提下,Lovable 在任何价格点上都不便宜。 非技术买家把 Lovable 对标到外包报价。一个基础网站或应用 3000–30000 美金。Lovable 月费 25–50 美金。非技术买家没有「LLM 包出来的产品够不到」的价格上限。他们买的是「我不用去找、考核、付款给外包」,这在一个项目上就省下 5000 美金。 案例说得直接:护城河在非技术买家。开发者市场拥挤且价格敏感。非技术买家市场大、竞争小、结构上愿意付费,因为他们的替代方案是「项目根本不做」。 案例点出的飞轮 案例给出三层叠加: 每周 100 万个新项目上线平台。 每个项目默认公开,每个项目就是一个营销面。 这些项目合计每月 6 亿次访问。 访问者看到被做出来的东西,品牌与信任一起涨,反哺 SEO。 800 万用户里过半用在能赚钱的活上。 这不是闲着试玩的人。这是在平台上跑真实生意的人,他们会为 Pro 和 Business 付费。 飞轮是「项目数 → 访问数 → 用户数 → 付费用户数」。四个环节没有哪个是新的。新的是四个环节在同一平台上同时跑,因为每个环节都在反哺下一个,所以很难复制。 「不做模型」的姿态 Lovable 不训自己的基础模型。案例说得很直白:它包的是第三方大模型。竞争壁垒是「包」——项目结构、提示词到应用的流程、素材库、部署管线。任何竞争者都能调同样的 API,但很少有人能以同样节奏交付同样质量的包。 这笔交换和之前那支

title: Lovable''s 500M USD ARR under 200 people: what the pricing curve hides
description: Sweden-based Lovable reportedly hit 500M USD ARR within two years of launch with under 200 employees and a 6.6B USD valuation. Cold read of the unit economics, what the pricing tiers imply, and why "vibe coding" is a non-technical-buyer product, not a developer tool.
excerpt: Lovable, a Sweden-based AI app builder, reportedly hit 500M USD ARR inside two years of launch with
body: Lovable, a Sweden-based AI app builder, reportedly hit 500M USD ARR inside two years of launch with under 200 employees and a 6.6B USD valuation. The product does not train its own model. It wraps third-party LLMs into a flow where the user describes what they want and gets a working full-stack app. The case below is a cold read of the unit economics and what the pricing tiers actually imply. The numbers Quantity Value Annualized revenue (latest) 500M USD Headcount <200 Revenue per employee 2M+ USD/year Valuation 6.6B USD Funding round 330M USD ARR growth (latest step) 25%+ Projects shipped per week 1M Monthly visits across shipped apps 600M Total users 8M (50%+ using it for revenue work) The figures are company-attributed. Not audited. Useful as a directional argument. The pricing tiers The case names three tiers. The shape is more important than the numbers: The meter is project complexity and number of generated artifacts. This is "ship a starter, then meter usage" — the same shape as Vercel, the same shape as cloud egress, the same shape as any usage-priced infrastructure product. The starter is free, the user's first project is free, and the price scales with how much the user

title: Medvi 案例：2 个人的远程医疗公司如何在 16 个月里做出 4 亿美金销售额，把 2442 人的对手甩在 5.5% 净利率
description: 深度拆解 Medvi——一个 2 人远程医疗公司如何在 16 个月里做到 4 亿美金销售额、16% 净利率，2442 人的对手 Hims and Hers 反而只有 5.5%。"拥有客户、租用其他"论点、AI 工具栈算账、模型在哪里断。
excerpt: Matthew Gallagher 在 2024 年 9 月用 2 万美金、零员工开了 Medvi。16 个月后这家公司做了 4 亿美金销售额、25 万客户、付两份工资、净利率 16.2%。对手 Hi
body: Matthew Gallagher 在 2024 年 9 月用 2 万美金、零员工开了 Medvi。16 个月后这家公司做了 4 亿美金销售额、25 万客户、付两份工资、净利率 16.2%。对手 Hims and Hers 有 2442 个员工、24 亿美金营收、净利率 5.5%。案例论断：差距不是 AI vs 人，是拥有客户 vs 拥有基础设施。下面是账——哪里站得住、哪里被夸。 头条数字，并排放 指标 Medvi（2 人） Hims and Hers（2442 人） 成立 2024 年 9 月 2017 年 2025 销售额 约 4 亿美金 约 24 亿美金 服务客户 约 25 万 案例未披露 员工 2（创始人 + 弟弟） 2442 净利率 16.2% 5.5% 2026 预期 约 18 亿美金 案例未披露 人均销售：Medvi 2 亿美金/人，Hims and Hers 约 100 万美金/人。200 倍差距，案例解读为"人不是资产，客户关系才是"。数字均为公司自报，无公开审计。 "拥有 vs 租用"的拆分 Medvi 拥有： 品牌和客户面身份 广告素材和分发渠道 客户关系和复购环 AI 辅助的客服层 定价和包装 Medvi 租用： 第三方合规平台上的持牌医生 药房履约和最后一公里配送 支付 客服溢出 AI 工具（Claude 写内容、语音合成撑呼叫中心、编程助手迭代产品） 创始人是"客户获取和体验层"，其余是基础设施。案例论断："租"列里的每一项都是一个 SaaS 或服务生意，API 成熟、SLA 成熟、价格线性。"拥有"列才是出 margin 的那部分。 AI 工具栈的账 10-40 倍成本差就是护城河。接单者吃差价，客户付得比正规渠道少，租来的基础设施吃掉牌照和合规的活。案例的点：AI 不是产品，AI 是让一个人干十个人活的杠杆。 为什么"这不是 AI 替代医生"的故事 案例写得很清楚：医生本来就不在内部。医疗人力本来就是外包给第三方合规平台的。AI 压的是客户体验层的成本，不是医疗层的成本。试图"替代医生"的人会撞上 FDA / 州医委会的墙；拥有客户、租用医生的接单者既合法又有 margin。 结构上的功课是看任何一个受监管行业，问：哪一层是有牌照的，哪一层是关系？拥有关系、租牌照。同样的形状在 legaltech（拥有 intake，租律师）、会计（拥有 workflow，租 CPA）、教育（拥有课程销售，租老师）都能用。 个人接单者剧本 案例末尾给了一份 6 条清单： 决定你"拥有"什么。品牌、客户名单、复购环、分发。这些绝不外包。 其余全租。合规、履约、基础设施、支付、甚至客服溢出都有成熟的 SaaS，别自己重建。 用现成积木搭系统。支付、邮件、语音、AI 工具都有生产级方案，拼而不是造。 把广告投放做成可测试的机器。AI 一天出 50

title: Medvi case: how a 2-person telehealth company hit 400M USD sales in 16 months by owning customers and renting everything else
description: Cold-eyed read of Medvi, a 2-person remote-health operator that cleared 400M USD in 16 months and 16% net margin while its 2,442-person competitor ran at 5.5%. The "own the customer, rent the rest" thesis, the AI tool stack math, and where the model breaks.
excerpt: Matthew Gallagher opened Medvi in September 2024 with 20K USD and no employees. Sixteen months later
body: Matthew Gallagher opened Medvi in September 2024 with 20K USD and no employees. Sixteen months later the company had done 400M USD in sales, served 250K customers, paid two salaries, and posted a 16.2% net margin. The competitor (Hims and Hers) had 2,442 employees, 2.4B USD in sales, and a 5.5% net margin. The case argues the gap is not AI vs human; it is owning the customer vs owning the infrastructure. Numbers, math, and where the model breaks. The headline numbers, side by side Metric Medvi (2-person) Hims and Hers (2,442-person) Founded September 2024 2017 2025 sales 400M USD 2.4B USD Customers served 250,000 not disclosed in this case Employees 2 (founder + brother) 2,442 Net margin 16.2% 5.5% 2026 trajectory 1.8B USD not disclosed in this case Per-employee sales: Medvi at 200M USD/employee, Hims and Hers at 1M USD/employee. A 200x gap that the case reads as "the headcount was not the asset — the customer relationship was." Numbers are self-reported; no audited statements are public. The "own vs rent" split What Medvi owns: Brand and customer-facing identity Ad creative and distribution channels Customer relationship and repeat-purchase loop AI-assisted customer support layer

title: Medvi: 2 people, 400M USD in 15 months, and the 'own the customer, lease the rest' model behind the curve
description: Cold read of Medvi (Matthew Gallagher): 2-person remote-health company doing 400M USD in sales on 250k customers in year one, scaling toward 1.8B USD, 16.2% net margin vs Hims at 5.5% with 2,442 staff. The structural claim: the company is not a healthcare company — it is a customer-acquisition and experience layer on top of rented infrastructure.
excerpt: A knowledge-planet case profiles Matthew Gallagher's Medvi: a US remote-health company that reported
body: A knowledge-planet case profiles Matthew Gallagher's Medvi: a US remote-health company that reportedly hit 400M USD in sales on 250,000 customers in 15 months with a 2-person team, tracking toward 1.8B USD in 2026. The structural claim the case lands on is not "AI replaced the doctors" — it is "Medvi is not a healthcare company. It is a customer-acquisition and experience layer that sits on top of rented healthcare infrastructure." Below is the math, the leased-stack model, and four gaps the case does not address. What the case lays out Quantity Value Founder Matthew Gallagher, 41, LA Co-founders 1 (his brother) Total team 2 Launch Sep 2024 Reported sales (year 1) 400M USD Reported customers 250,000 2026 trajectory 1.8B USD (self-reported projection) Net margin 16.2% Comparable: Hims and Hers 2.4B USD revenue, 2,442 employees, 5.5% net margin Product focus Cash-pay prescription weight-loss, direct-to-consumer The case treats the team-size-to-revenue ratio as the load-bearing proof point, not the headline revenue number itself. What Medvi owns vs leases The structural distinction the case draws: A full-stack telehealth company would own all of the leased items — clinical staff, phar

title: Medvi:2 个人,15 个月做 4 亿美金,以及「持有客户、租用其余」模型怎么撑起这条曲线
description: 拆解 Medvi(Matthew Gallagher):2 人远程健康公司 15 个月做到 4 亿美金销售、25 万客户,正冲 18 亿;净利率 16.2%,对比 Hims 5.5% 配 2442 个员工。结构主张:这家公司不是一家医疗公司——它是建在租用基础设施之上的客户获取与体验层。
excerpt: 一条知识星球帖子拆的是 Matthew Gallagher 的 Medvi:一家美国远程医疗公司,据报 15 个月做到 4 亿美金销售、25 万客户,2 人团队,2026 年冲 18 亿。帖子最终落到
body: 一条知识星球帖子拆的是 Matthew Gallagher 的 Medvi:一家美国远程医疗公司,据报 15 个月做到 4 亿美金销售、25 万客户,2 人团队,2026 年冲 18 亿。帖子最终落到的结构主张不是「AI 替代了医生」——是「Medvi 不是一家医疗公司,它是建在租用医疗基础设施之上的客户获取与体验层」。下面是数学、租用栈模型、以及案例没展开的四点。 案例说的数字 数量 值 创始人 Matthew Gallagher,41 岁,洛杉矶 联合创始人 1(他弟弟) 团队总数 2 上线 2024 年 9 月 公开销售(第一年) 4 亿美金 公开客户数 25 万 2026 跟踪 18 亿美金(自报预测) 净利率 16.2% 可比公司:Hims and Hers 24 亿美金收入、2442 名员工、5.5% 净利率 产品方向 现金支付处方减肥药,D2C 案例把「团队规模对收入」的比率当成承重的证据点,不是把标题收入本身。 Medvi 持有 vs 租用什么 案例画的结构分界: 一家全栈远程医疗公司会把租用项全部持有——临床员工、药房基础设施、客服团队、履约。Medvi 一项都不持有。「持有客户、租用其余」是 headcount 卡在 2 人的原因。 为什么这个模型能产生 3 倍净利率 Hims and Hers Health——最接近的公开可比公司——约 24 亿美金收入、2442 名员工、5.5% 净利率。Medvi 据报 16.2% 净利率 + 2 人团队。净利率差不是「公司更小」的函数,是成本结构的函数: 成本项 全栈远程医疗 Medvi 临床员工 雇佣医生,约 20 万美金 / 年 × N 按次租用,随收入可变 药房 自有牌照、履约中心 按张租用,随收入可变 客服 内部团队,约 4 万美金 / 年 × N AI + 语音合成,约 5000–10000 美金 / 月 资本支出 诊所、牌照、履约建设 零 工具栈 per-employee 工具 AI 工具 3000–12000 美金 / 年替代 13 万美金 + 人力 客服这条 3–10 倍的成本差,就是 16.2% 净利率在小团队下能成立的关键。临床与药房层随收入走可变成本,headcount 完全不需要随收入扩展。 价格锚 案例指出 Medvi 的定价锚的是人力成本,不是按张处方计费。一次美国远程问诊按服务报价 80–150 美金,在 Medvi 的订阅包里被打到 30–60 美金。客户把这个 bundle 比作「去诊所」,觉得便宜。Medvi 拿毛利,因为租用进来的边际问诊成本接近 5–15 美金。 这是 Cognition / Devin 同样的「岗位定价」动作,但套在了远程问诊而不是软件工程上。两个产品都落在客户 P&L 的人力那条线,不是按次服务的账单线。 案例点名的失败模式 案例对一条失

title: Mercor hits 2B USD run rate in 3 years by selling expert hours, not models: the pick-and-shovel math of the AI-training data supply chain
description: Mercor — 3 years old, 2B USD annualized revenue, 3.89M USD daily payouts to experts, 295K jobs created at 124 USD/hour average. The product is not a model and not an app — it is a staffing layer that matches domain experts (oncologists, lawyers, MBB consultants) to AI-training tasks. Pricing anchored against expert hours, not software seats. The pick-and-shovel position, the expert-supply economics, and the four gaps the case does not cover, below.
excerpt: Mercor — a staffing layer between AI labs and domain experts. 2B USD annualized revenue by June 2026
body: Mercor — a staffing layer between AI labs and domain experts. 2B USD annualized revenue by June 2026. 1B USD annualized in February 2026 (4 months earlier). 500M USD annualized in September 2025 (5 months earlier). 295K expert jobs created. 3.89M USD paid out daily. Average expert rate 124 USD/hour. Top rates: oncology 130–180 USD/hour, legal 100–150 USD/hour, MBB consulting 100 USD/hour. The interesting part is not 'Mercor hires experts' — it is the pick-and-shovel position above every model lab. The unit economics, the expert-supply moat, and the four gaps the case does not cover, below. The pick-and-shovel position The 2B USD annualized claim is most likely GMV / billings, not net revenue. At a 30% take rate on 366M USD in expert billings, Mercor's net revenue is closer to 110M USD — still a real business, but a different number. The case presents the bigger number because that is what reads well in growth-stage metrics. Why the moat is operational, not technical The moat is not the matching algorithm. Three operational layers compound: Recruitment. Mercor's brand attracts experts who want flexible hourly work. The supply side is widening month over month. A new entrant faces a

title: Norm AI 不卖法律软件,开律所 Norm Law:为什么「AI 律师工具」框定错了,「AI 律师值班的律所」才是对的
description: Norm AI。2023 年 7 月 John Nay 创办。C 轮:120 万美金/1.2B 估值,累计融资 2.6 亿+。客户管理 30 万亿+ 美金资产——黑石、贝恩、先锋、花旗、纽约人寿。Norm 不是法律软件公司。Norm 开着一家律所——Norm Law;AI agent 是律师,人类资深律师监督。客户付法律服务费,不付软件订阅。框定是「律所」,不是「工具」——律所按案件计费,工具按席位计费。
excerpt: 一条知识星球帖子拆的是 Norm AI,John Nay 在 2023 年 7 月创办。C 轮:1.2 亿美金,12 亿估值。累计融资 2.6 亿+。客户管理 30 万亿+ 美金资产——黑石、贝恩资本
body: 一条知识星球帖子拆的是 Norm AI,John Nay 在 2023 年 7 月创办。C 轮:1.2 亿美金,12 亿估值。累计融资 2.6 亿+。客户管理 30 万亿+ 美金资产——黑石、贝恩资本、先锋、花旗、纽约人寿。这条不在技术,在结构:Norm 不卖法律软件产品。Norm 开着一家律所——Norm Law。AI agent 做法律工作(合同审阅、监管分析、合规检查);人类资深律师监督、校准、纠错。客户把 Norm Law 当外部法律顾问聘。交付物是法律意见,不是软件输出。重点在计费模型——律所按案件计费,工具按席位计费。下面是结构框定、按客户数学,以及案例没展开的四点。 结构框定 重点不是「Norm 的 AI 更强」,是:Norm 开着律所,不是工具。律所的天花板是客户的 workload;工具的天花板是客户的 headcount。对一家「法律 workload 远大于法务 headcount」的企业客户,律所框定是天花板更高的营收模型。 客户名单 客户 管理 AUM / 资产 隐含合同规模(粗略) 黑石 1.1T 美金 AUM 2M–10M 美金/年 贝恩资本 185B 美金 AUM 1M–5M 美金/年 先锋 8T 美金 AUM 5M–20M 美金/年 花旗 2.4T 美金资产 5M–20M 美金/年 纽约人寿 700B 美金资产 2M–10M 美金/年 合计 30T+ 隐含 20M–100M 美金/年 book 案例没给按客户营收。隐含范围与 50M–150M ARR 公司一致。客户集中度是那个没说出的变量——少数几家财富级合同大概率占 book 主体。 单位经济 案例没给监督比,这是核心单位经济变量。1:10 是软件级毛利(80%+);1:2 是律所级毛利(40–50%)。两者之间公司估值差 2 倍。 为什么 2026 年「律所」是对的框定 这点和 Cognition 一样:律所框定移除了按席位营收天花板。法律 AI 工具席位市场是红海(Harvey、CoCounsel、Spellbook)。「AI 值班的律所产能」市场没那么多竞品——因为大多数法律 AI 厂不想吃律所形态带来的无照执业风险。 律所框定也是唯一能撑住 12 亿估值的框定。50M ARR 的按席位法律 AI 工具,估值 250M–500M。100M ARR 的律所形态,毛利是律所级,估值 1B–2B。律所框定是撑住这个倍数的那个。 案例没说的事 四点。 杠杆比。 1 资深律师带几个 agent。这是核心单位经济变量,案例没说。1:10 是软件级;1:2 是律所级。两者毛利与倍数差异很大。 收费结构。 固定费 vs 按小时。律所按小时计费是 cost-plus 生意;按案件固定费是 value-based 生意。案例没给。 执业责任。 AI agent 给错法律意见,谁背 ma

title: Norm AI doesn't sell legal software, it runs Norm Law: why 'AI lawyer tool' is the wrong frame and 'AI-staffed law firm' is the right one
description: A look at Norm AI. Founded July 2023 by John Nay. Series C: $120M at $1.2B valuation, total funding $260M. Customers manage over $30T in assets — Blackstone, Bain, Vanguard, Citi, New York Life. Norm is not a legal-software company. Norm runs a law firm called Norm Law; the AI agents are the lawyers, human senior lawyers supervise. The customer pays for the legal service, not the software subscription. The frame is 'firm', not 'tool' — and a firm bills by the matter, not by the seat.
excerpt: A case profiles Norm AI, founded by John Nay in July 2023. Series C: $120M raised at a $1.2B valuati
body: A case profiles Norm AI, founded by John Nay in July 2023. Series C: $120M raised at a $1.2B valuation. Total funding: $260M+. Customers manage over $30T in assets — Blackstone, Bain Capital, Vanguard, Citi, New York Life. The interesting part is not the technology but the structure: Norm does not sell a legal-software product. Norm runs a law firm called Norm Law. AI agents do the legal work (contract review, regulatory analysis, compliance checks); human senior lawyers supervise, calibrate, correct. The customer engages Norm Law as outside counsel. The deliverable is legal advice, not software output. The billing model is the point — a firm bills by the matter, a tool bills by the seat. The structural framing, per-customer math, and four gaps the case does not address, below. The frame The case is not 'Norm has better AI'. The case is: Norm operates a firm, not a tool. A firm's ceiling is the customer's workload; a tool's ceiling is the customer's headcount. For an enterprise customer whose legal workload is much larger than its legal headcount, the firm frame is the higher-ceiling revenue model. The customer roster Customer AUM / assets managed Implied deal size (rough) Blacksto

title: PayPal vs Stripe 手续费对比器
description: 输入客单价和月单量，对比 PayPal 与 Stripe 的月手续费总额和有效费率。
excerpt: PayPal 和 Stripe 在标准卡交易上都收大约 3% + $0.30，但一旦叠加国际卡、货币转换、拒付费，结构差异立刻拉开。一个 $35 客单价 × 300 单/月的店铺，月流水 $10,50
body: PayPal 和 Stripe 在标准卡交易上都收大约 3% + $0.30，但一旦叠加国际卡、货币转换、拒付费，结构差异立刻拉开。一个 $35 客单价 × 300 单/月的店铺，月流水 $10,500——1% 有效费率差就是 $1,260/年。 计算器怎么算 客单价 (USD)——平均订单金额。 月单量——每月订单总数。 输出： 各家月手续费 有效费率 = 月手续费 ÷ 月流水 差额——按月的金额差 默认假设 PayPal 商业交易（4.4% + $0.30）和 Stripe 标准卡（2.9% + $0.30 基础，含约 1.5% 跨境卡附加作为综合有效费率）。按自己的实际客单组成调整。 不同订单量的有效费率 客单价 月单量 流水 PayPal 费 Stripe 费 月差 年差 $25 200 $5,000 $280 $194 $86 $1,032 $35 300 $10,500 $569 $404 $165 $1,980 $60 500 $30,000 $1,590 $1,140 $450 $5,400 $120 150 $18,000 $948 $696 $252 $3,024 差额与流水大致线性相关，与单数无关。$30k/月流水，PayPal 多收 $5,400/年——在某些市场够一个全职员工。Stripe 在标准国内卡场景胜出；PayPal 只在买家明确拒绝刷卡的场景才赢。 这工具不算什么 拒付 / 争议费（每笔 $15–20）——预留准备金。 订阅计费折扣——Stripe Billing 和 PayPal Subscriptions 的留存卡定价不一样。 本地支付（iDEAL、Klarna、Alipay）——Adyen、Mollie、Airwallex Payment Acceptance 等聚合器用一笔费率包打。 刷卡 vs 输卡——POS 与线上定价不同。 常见错误 把"无月费"等同于免费——PayPal 无月费但单笔费率更高，跑数看看。 忽略 1.5% 国际附加费——在基础费率上叠加。PayPal 跨境交易 4.4% + 1.5% = 5.9%，还没算汇损。 忘记货币转换——客户付 EUR、你结算 USD，PayPal 在公示汇率上再加 3–4%。Stripe 是 1%。 低估拒付——准备 0.1–0.3% 流水的准备金；高客单品类（电子、保健品）可能到 1%。 何时切换服务商 <$50k/月 + 纯国内：Stripe 通常便宜 0.5–1%。PayPal 的支票罕见。 跨境销售：Stripe 银行间汇率比 PayPal 内嵌汇损便宜 1–2%。 B2B 或年长欧盟客户：Stripe 为主，PayPal 留为备用——结账转化常常提升。 订阅业务：Stripe Billing 的留存卡费率显著低于 PayPal Subscriptions（规

body: Both PayPal and Stripe charge roughly 3% + $0.30 on standard card transactions, but the structures diverge quickly once you add international cards, currency conversion, and dispute fees. A $35 product on 300 orders/month generates $10,500 in revenue — a 1% effective-rate difference is $1,260/year. How this calculator works Unit price (USD) — average order value. Adjust the inputs to your actual mix. title: PayPal vs Stripe Fees in 2026: The Real Cost Per Sale
description: Side-by-side fee comparison of PayPal and Stripe for cross-border sellers. Includes micro-fee trap, chargeback handling, and currency conversion costs.
excerpt: PayPal and Stripe look similar on paper. The real cost difference shows up on international sales an
body: PayPal and Stripe look similar on paper. The real cost difference shows up on international sales and currency conversion. Standard US card fees (2026) Feature PayPal Checkout Stripe Online transaction 3.49% + $0.49 2.9% + $0.30 In-person (card present) 2.29% + $0.09 2.7% + $0.05 Monthly fee $0 $0 Setup fee $0 $0 Chargeback fee $20 $15 PayPal's standard online rate is 3.49% + $0.49 (often quoted as "2.99% + $0.49" — that's the older rate). Stripe is 2.9% + $0.30 for the first $1M/year processed. The difference on a $50 sale: PayPal: $2.24 Stripe: $1.75 You keep $0.49 more per sale with Stripe. The micro-fee trap Both processors charge a fixed fee per transaction. For small-ticket items, this is a huge percentage: Sale price Stripe cost As % of sale $5 $0.45 8.9% $10 $0.59 5.9% $25 $1.03 4.1% $50 $1.75 3.5% $100 $3.20 3.2% Below $10, processor fees are over 6% of revenue. If you sell small items, either bundle them or switch to a flat-rate processor like Square (2.6% + $0.10 in-person, 2.9% + $0.30 online). title: Postiz 案例:30+ 社交渠道 + 开源,撑起 14.5 万美金 MRR,跟主流赛道错开的那条路
description: 拆解 Postiz(开源社媒排期工具)走到 14.5 万美金 MRR 的路子。它真正变现的是什么,护城河在哪里,案例没说清楚哪几件事,以及「长尾集成 + 开源」单位经济学的拆解。
excerpt: 一个知识星球帖子抛出一个常见误区：做工具型产品，上来就想做一个「更好用的版本」，结果掉进最挤的赛道跟现有玩家抢同一批客户。案例拆的是一款独立开发者做的开源社媒排期工具，据报已跨过 14.5 万美金 M
body: 一个知识星球帖子抛出一个常见误区：做工具型产品，上来就想做一个「更好用的版本」，结果掉进最挤的赛道跟现有玩家抢同一批客户。案例拆的是一款独立开发者做的开源社媒排期工具，据报已跨过 14.5 万美金 MRR，做法是接 30+ 发布渠道，包括主流工具懒得做的长尾平台。下面是数字、承重部分，以及案例没说的事。 案例描述的内容 数量 值(自报) 产品 Postiz(开源社媒排期) 公开 MRR 14.5 万美金 集成数 30+(主流 + Mastodon、Bluesky、Nostr、Warpcast、Telegram、Discord 等长尾) 许可证 开源 + hosted 付费订阅 团队规模 单个独立开发者(按案例口吻) 数字由创始人自报，作为方向性参考；案例用它们去论证「开源 + 长尾」的组合是承重策略，不是功能集。 为什么 30+ 集成是护城河 功能型护城河（更聪明的 UI、更干净的 dashboard、更精致的模板）半衰期只有几周。下一个版本竞品就追上来了，差异化消失。 集成型护城河累积而不衰减： 每加一个集成，带来三样东西： 新买家池。 每个集成都是一个长尾搜索词（「post to Mastodon + scheduler」)。SEO 自然增长，不烧钱。 涨价锚点。 老订阅者看到 dashboard 里不断多出来的新集成，「同等月费下价值上升」，留存自动加固，不需要急着提价。 迁移税。 一旦客户把 20+ 渠道都接进了某个排期器，评估竞品时迁移代价（重新走 20 遍 OAuth、配权限、回放队列）是真实的。切换成本随集成宽度增长。 到了 30+ 集成，竞品 90 天内追不上来。这才是护城河。 为什么开源是获客渠道 案例把开源框架解释成免费获客。三套机制： GitHub star 当 SEO。 1 万–2 万 star 的仓库，自然排在 「social scheduler github」 搜索上，每一个 star 都是一条被动外链，流向自部署的漏斗。营销预算接近零。 自部署者当布道者。 一家小代理自己部署 Postiz、服务 5 个客户，就是一个 5 家客户的推荐漏斗。不会主动投放，但会跟同行说。漏斗的获客成本是这位执行安装的人，不是付费投放。 贡献者当集成工程师。 Mastodon 集成需要 patch 时，这是个开源工件，会有社区贡献者接手，而不是创始人自己背带宽。在不发工资的前提下，工程带宽靠社区放大。 开源 / 不开源的选择，本质上是营销动作。Hosted 计划才是钱，自部署是漏斗。 14.5 万美金 MRR 的算术 对一个独立 SaaS，这个数字落在顶级分布。这个品类的大多数独立工具一辈子没跨过 2–3 万 MRR。14.5 万这个数，意味着这位独立开发者要么（a）花了 4 年以上死磕集成宽度，(b）拿到了能压过按席位数学的企业 / 代理 tier

title: Postiz 单人做到 14.5 万美金 MRR:集成 30 个别人懒得接的平台才是护城河
description: 一支单人开发的社媒排期工具,据披露月经常性收入做到 14.5 万美金,秘诀是接了 30 多个发布渠道——包括竞品懒得接的 Mastodon、Bluesky、Nostr、Warpcast、Telegram、Discord。下面拆「渠道数」为什么比「更好的 UI」更重要,以及开源怎么变成付费转化漏斗。
excerpt: 一支单人开发的社媒排期工具 Postiz 据披露月经常性收入做到 14.5 万美金。产品不是靠 UI 精致或功能深度赢的。它赢在「接了 30 多个发布渠道」——包括竞品觉得「用户太少不值得做」的 Ma
body: 一支单人开发的社媒排期工具 Postiz 据披露月经常性收入做到 14.5 万美金。产品不是靠 UI 精致或功能深度赢的。它赢在「接了 30 多个发布渠道」——包括竞品觉得「用户太少不值得做」的 Mastodon、Bluesky、Nostr、Warpcast、Telegram、Discord。整套产品还开源。下面拆「渠道数」为什么是护城河,以及开源怎么变成付费转化漏斗。 数字 数量 值 MRR(最新) 14.5 万美金 推算 ARR 174 万美金 发布渠道 30+ 代码许可 开源 融资 自负盈亏(无轮次披露) 团队 单人 founder 创始人自报、未审计。数字是策略的方向锚,不是财报。 为什么 30 个渠道是护城河 案例点得很清楚。一个 6 渠道的排期工具,处在和其他 6 渠道排期工具的功能对等赛跑里。买家比的是 UI、价格、集成数。工具至少要赢其中一项才能转化。 一个 30 渠道的排期工具不在同一场比赛里。买家把它对标到「雇一个兼职社媒运营,去那 24 个平台手动发」。对标框架从「功能对等」滑到「替代人工」。价格上限从「我愿意为排期工具付多少」跳到「我愿意为不做这些活付多少」。 机械原因是:每个集成是一个第三方 API 加上鉴权流加上格式适配器。集成成本真实存在——一个能用的集成大约 3–10 个工程日。一个 30 渠道的工具代表 90–300 工程日的集成工作。新入场者要花同样的时间才能追平。每多接一个渠道,护城河就厚一分。 开源怎么变成付费漏斗 案例描述了一个三步循环: 开源仓库。 GitHub star 累积起来,反哺可见度。自部署用户装上开始用。 自部署用户撞上运维极限。 一些用户规模超过自部署的舒适区(数据库备份、可用性、版本升级、安全补丁)。他们开始找托管版。 托管版就是付费产品。 自部署用户是预审过的线索,因为他们已经在工作流里验证过产品。转化率结构性地高于冷流量。 案例说得很直白:开源不是慷慨。开源是一条免费的分销渠道,把预审过的线索交到你手上。托管付费产品是这层之上的变现。 定价锚点 案例暗示 Postiz 定价在 30–80 美金/月。锚的是替代人工,不是同行订阅价。 一个兼职社媒运营在大多数市场 1000–2000 美金/月。Postiz 50 美金/月替代大约每月 10 小时的发布工作。买家比的是 50 美金/月 对 1500 美金/月的人工,30 倍价值比。价格上限结构性地高于任何把锚定在 Buffer 或 Hootsuite 定价上的「排期工具」竞争者。 这和之前法律 AI 案例同形:把价格锚到工具替代的人力成本上,而不是同行软件月费上。价格上限从软件预算跑到 HR 预算。 复用的路径 案例暗示单人 founder 想复用结构,有四步: 挑一个现有玩家只覆盖 5–6 个渠道或工作流的品类。 自己覆盖 15+ 长尾。品类不一定

title: Postiz case: 145,000 USD MRR from 30+ social channels and an open-source spine — a builder''s playbook for leaving the mainstream lane
description: Cold read of Postiz (open-source social scheduling tool) crossing 145,000 USD MRR. What it actually monetizes, where its moat sits, what the source quietly skips, and the unit economics of going long-tail instead of competing for mainstream channels.
excerpt: A knowledge-planet post calls out a familiar mistake: builders of tool products start by trying to s
body: A knowledge-planet post calls out a familiar mistake: builders of tool products start by trying to ship "a better version" of an existing tool, which drops them into the most crowded lane competing for the same pool of buyers as the incumbent. The case walks through an open-source social-scheduling tool built by an indie developer that reportedly crossed 145,000 USD MRR by doing something different: it shipped integrations to 30+ publishing channels, including the long-tail ones mainstream tools refuse to integrate. Below is the math, what is load-bearing, and what the case is silent on. What the case describes Quantity Value (self-reported) Product Postiz (open-source social scheduling) Reported MRR 145,000 USD Integrations 30+ (mainstream + long-tail: Mastodon, Bluesky, Nostr, Warpcast, Telegram, Discord, etc.) Licensing Open source + hosted paid plans Architecture Single indie developer (per the case framing) All numbers are founder-attributed. Treat them as advisory; the case uses them to argue the open-source-plus-long-tail combination is the load-bearing strategy, not the feature set. Why 30+ integrations is the moat A feature-style moat (smarter UI, cleaner dashboard, better

title: Product Pricing Calculator for Cross-border Sellers
description: Free Shopify pricing calculator. Enter cost, shipping, fees and target margin — get the suggested price, profit per unit and break-even price instantly.
excerpt: The Product Pricing Calculator answers the question every cross-border seller asks: what should I ch
body: The Product Pricing Calculator answers the question every cross-border seller asks: what should I charge to actually make money? Most pricing formulas stop at "cost × markup." That misses shipping, platform commission, payment processing, and the small per-order fixed fees that quietly eat your margin. This calculator includes all four. How to use it Enter your product cost (what you pay the supplier, in USD). Add your shipping cost per order (or use 0 for free-shipping models). Enter commission % (Shopify Payments is 2.9% in the US; Etsy is 6.5% + 3%; Amazon is 15%). Enter payment processing % (often the same as commission if your platform bundles it). Add any fixed per-order fee (Shopify charges $0.30; PayPal charges $0.49 in some regions). Set your target net margin (after all fees). Read the suggested price, break-even price, and profit per unit. The formula The break-even price assumes 0% net margin — anything you sell above it is profit. Worked example A $8 product with $3.50 shipping, 2.9% commission, 2.9% payment fee, $0.30 fixed fee, and a 30% target margin: Suggested price: $24.85 Break-even price: $13.05 (zero profit) Profit per unit at the suggested price: $7.46 If you

title: ROAS 保本与目标利润率计算器
description: 根据客单价、成本、支付费率和目标利润率，反算保本 ROAS、目标 ROAS 和最高 CPA。
excerpt: ROAS（广告支出回报率）是付费社交里最常用的指标，但多数卖家只是凭感觉看，不去验算。3.0x ROAS 听起来很好，拆开算利润率——8% 支付费、25% 目标利润率下，保本其实只有 1.85x。低于
body: ROAS（广告支出回报率）是付费社交里最常用的指标，但多数卖家只是凭感觉看，不去验算。3.0x ROAS 听起来很好，拆开算利润率——8% 支付费、25% 目标利润率下，保本其实只有 1.85x。低于这个数，首单就亏钱。 计算器怎么算 客单价 (USD)——售价（打折后实际成交价）。 成本 + 物流 (USD)——到岸价，含生产、包装、运到客户手中的运费。 支付费率 (%)——总处理费，含汇损和平台佣金。 目标利润率 (%)——扣完所有固定成本后想要的净利润率。 输出： 保本 ROAS = 1 ÷ (1 − 支付费率% − 目标利润率%)。低于这个数，每个广告美元都亏。 目标 ROAS——达到目标利润率所需的倍数。 最高 CPA——首单保本情况下你最多能付的获客成本。 参考场景 客单价 成本+物流 支付费率 目标利润率 保本 目标 ROAS 最高 CPA $25 $8 4% 20% 1.32x 1.65x $16.00 $45 $14 4% 25% 1.41x 1.85x $29.20 $80 $22 4% 30% 1.52x 2.16x $54.80 $150 $45 5% 35% 1.67x 2.56x $97.50 $300 $90 5% 40% 1.82x 3.03x $195.00 保本随目标利润率变化慢；最高 CPA 随客单价线性变化。最容易把广告搞崩的杠杆是支付费率漂移（汇损附加、支付商分级调整）——有效费率涨 1%，需要多 10% 的 ROAS 来补偿。 超越 ROAS：四个杠杆 ROAS 是输出。输入的杠杆是： CTR（点击率）——由素材和开场决定。 CVR（转化率）——由落地页、优惠、定价锚点决定。 AOV（客单价）——套装、加购、临界包邮。 CPM（千次曝光成本）——由受众质量和竞争决定。 ROAS 不达标时，按顺序排查：先看 CPM（竞价问题？）、再看 CTR（素材疲劳？）、最后查 CVR（落地页不匹配？）。 常见错误 跨归因窗口比 ROAS——Facebook 7 天点击 vs Google 30 天点击不能直接比。先锁窗口再 benchmark。 忽略退货和拒付——它们让成本涨两次（退货 + 丢失的获客成本）。目标利润率再加 2–5% 吸收它们。 忘记素材疲劳——14 天内 ROAS 跌 30% 但 CPM 没变，多半是素材废了，不是受众废了。 用 LTV 算首单——保本是首单。LTV 数学只在有复购数据时用，否则你在放大不盈利的群组。 把"目标 ROAS"当保证值——它是按你设的利润率算出来的倍数，不是广告实际能跑到的数。 何时这工具不够用 订阅或 LTV 业务——用群组 LTV / CAC 代替首单 ROAS。 高退货率品类（服装、美妆）——把退货率算进利润率假设；20% 退货会吃掉 25% 的目标利润。 B2B 长周期销售—

title: ROAS Break-Even Calculator
description: Compute break-even ROAS, target ROAS, and max CPA from your unit price, cost, payment fee and target margin.
excerpt: ROAS (Return on Ad Spend) is the single most-used metric in paid social, but most sellers eyeball it
body: ROAS (Return on Ad Spend) is the single most-used metric in paid social, but most sellers eyeball it without checking the math. A 3.0x ROAS sounds great until your margins and fees break it down — at 8% payment fee and 25% target margin, your break-even is closer to 1.85x. Anything below that loses money on the first order. How this calculator works Unit price (USD) — selling price (post-discount if you run promos). COGS + shipping (USD) — landed cost including production, packaging, freight to customer. Payment fee (%) — total processor fee including FX and platform commission. Target margin (%) — net margin you want after all fixed costs. The tool returns: Break-even ROAS = 1 / (1 − payment% − margin%). Below this, every ad dollar loses money. Target ROAS — multiplier needed to hit your target margin after ad spend. Max CPA — the most you can pay per acquired customer while staying break-even on order one. Reference scenarios Unit price COGS+ship Payment % Target margin Break-even Target ROAS Max CPA $25 $8 4% 20% 1.32x 1.65x $16.00 $45 $14 4% 25% 1.41x 1.85x $29.20 $80 $22 4% 30% 1.52x 2.16x $54.80 $150 $45 5% 35% 1.67x 2.56x $97.50 $300 $90 5% 40% 1.82x 3.03x $195.00 Break-even

title: SalesRobot: 一个 100 万美金 ARR 的 LinkedIn AI 工具, 4100 个客户, 零融资
description: LinkedIn 自动化 + 冷邮件 AI 工具, 100 万美金 ARR, 4100+ B2B 团队, 零外部资金。深度拆解: 单元经济, "高度分散市场"论, 以及 bootstrapped AI SaaS 真正证明了什么。
excerpt: SalesRobot 是 B2B 销售团队的 LinkedIn 自动化和冷邮件工具。创始人公开口径 100 万美金 ARR, 4100+ B2B 销售团队和获客代理作为客户, 零外部融资。创始人现在用
body: SalesRobot 是 B2B 销售团队的 LinkedIn 自动化和冷邮件工具。创始人公开口径 100 万美金 ARR, 4100+ B2B 销售团队和获客代理作为客户, 零外部融资。创始人现在用同一批客户做第二个产品 Kuron.ai。下面是单元经济, 让 bootstrap 能成立的结构性论点, 以及公开故事没讲的三个失败模式。 产品到底做什么 买家是 B2B 销售团队, 原本要付: 初级 SDR 综合 4000-6000 美金/月 外包 SDR 代理 3000-8000 美金/月每席 干冷外联的 VA 1500-2500 美金/月 工具干写作/时序/路由/多渠道协调, 人干 close。被替换的单位是客户 P&L 上一条人头费, 不是预算表上一个软件 license。 100 万 ARR 后面的算账 这就是隐含的 blended ARPU。20 USD/月每团队, SalesRobot 定价是自助式 SaaS, 不是企业级 seat。靠量。SalesRobot 不是 Salesforce, 定价姿态更接近 Calendly 或 Loom。 有意思的结构性观察: 创始人没融资, 但规模做到 1M ARR, 4100+ 客户。邻近赛道拿融资的对手客户数 50-200, ARPU 高 10-30 倍。不同的游戏, 不同的计分板。bootstrap 在客户广度上赢, 不在单客营收上赢。 "高度分散市场"这个论断 创始人押注的结构性论点: 市场 动态 对 AI 工具的含义 B2B 销售工具 买家是销售团队, 每家有自己 ICP/动作/行业合规 工具不能一刀切, 垂直深度的工具赢 LinkedIn 自动化 LinkedIn 是一个渠道, 买家平行用 3-5 个渠道 拿 LinkedIn 这一块的工具是更大 stack 的一小部分 冷邮件 买家散布在 100+ 行业, 各国送达率规则不同 单一"冷邮件 AI" 不能服务所有买家 销售赋能 买卖动作在 SaaS, 代理, 金融, 医疗之间完全不同 横向"AI for sales" 输给垂直工具 含义: B2B 销售工具市场不是赢家通吃。多个 1M-10M ARR 工具能共存, 不需要互相打死。赢的工具是窄买家画像上做深度的, 不是试图服务所有人的。 这也是 bootstrap 理性的原因。拿融资的对手得讲 TAM 扩张和赢家通吃来撑估值。bootstrap 工具不用。两家公司在玩不同的游戏, 不同的计分板。 为什么创始人拒绝融资 公开报道显示创始人多次被劝融资, 都拒了。理由 (转述): 市场分散, 没有能撑得起稀释的 TAM 扩张打法。 产品已经盈利, 外部资本会强加"不惜一切增长"的行为, 跟市场不匹配。 宁愿用第一个产品的现金流做第二个产品, 也不愿拿钱丢掉对第二个产品的控制。 这是少见的"我不需要你的钱"立

title: SalesRobot: A 1M USD ARR LinkedIn AI Tool With 4,100 Customers and Zero Outside Funding
description: A LinkedIn-automation and cold-email AI tool, 1M USD ARR, 4,100+ B2B sales teams, zero outside capital. Cold-eyed read of the unit economics, the "highly fragmented" market thesis, and what a bootstrapped AI SaaS actually proves.
excerpt: SalesRobot is a LinkedIn-automation and cold-email tool for B2B sales teams. The founder publicly st
body: SalesRobot is a LinkedIn-automation and cold-email tool for B2B sales teams. The founder publicly states 1M USD annual recurring revenue, 4,100+ B2B sales teams and lead-gen agencies as customers, zero outside capital. The founder is now building a second product (Kuron.ai) on the same customer base. Below is the unit economics, the structural thesis that makes the bootstrap work, and the three failure modes the public story does not address. What the product does The buyer is a B2B sales team that would otherwise pay: A junior SDR at 4,000-6,000 USD/month all-in An outsourced SDR agency at 3,000-8,000 USD/month per seat A VA handling cold outreach at 1,500-2,500 USD/month The tool does the writing, the timing, the routing, and the multi-channel coordination. The human does the close. The unit being replaced is a headcount line on the customer's P&L, not a software license on a budget spreadsheet. The arithmetic behind 1M ARR That is the implied blended ARPU. At 20 USD/month per team, SalesRobot is priced as a self-serve SaaS tool, not as an enterprise seat. Volume is the play. The product is not Salesforce; it is closer to a Calendly or a Loom in pricing posture. The interesting s

title: Seedance 2.0 产品视频案例：从月薪 8000 的详情页设计师到月入 3 万的 AI 视频接单
description: 深度拆解一个网络流传的 AI 产品视频接单案例。背后的算账、海外报价区间、"现在是窗口期"的四项论据，以及它在哪里站得住、在哪里拉满。
excerpt: 一位杭州产品设计师 2026 年 1 月从详情页设计转用 Seedance 2.0 接产品视频单。月工资从 8K RMB 到 6 个月内约 3 万。案例列三个海外报价档、四条"为什么现在是窗口"的论据
body: 一位杭州产品设计师 2026 年 1 月从详情页设计转用 Seedance 2.0 接产品视频单。月工资从 8K RMB 到 6 个月内约 3 万。案例列三个海外报价档、四条"为什么现在是窗口"的论据。下面是账——哪些站得住、哪些被夸。 6 个月收入曲线（按案例） 月份 收入（人民币） 备注 1 2,000 10 个小单 2 5,000 老客户转介绍 3 10,000 一个品牌 20 条打包 6 30,000 「稳定」（自述） 曲线读作 freelance 典型 hockey stick 的前半段。1-2 月是亏损式报价（第一单 200 块）。3 月起量大但客单价并不高，6 月以后转介绍飞轮转起来。30K 是稳态而非高点——当作"这个订单密度下的平台期"，不是"封顶天花板"。 数字均为作者自报，没有贴发票。 真正有意思的是"成本结构重写" 帖子里最值得拆的不是"AI 视频现在行不行"，是这一段： 质量进步是噱头；单位经济翻转是因为几万块人力被压成几百块订阅费。在中小客户的电商主图视频、Listing 视频、TikTok Shop 商品卡这一段，客户是为交付截止时间和单价付费，不是为电影感付费——重写就够用。 海外三档（按案例引述） 档位 交付物 价格（美金） 入门包 60 秒产品视频，3 天交付 250 – 450 标准包 脚本协作 + 品牌定制 + 双尺寸 600 – 1,200 高端包 3-5 条套装 + 多语言字幕 1,500 – 3,500 框架：「交付时间是传统的 1/5，收费是传统的 1/3，你的时薪其实是传统 3 倍」。这只在按包报价时成立——按小时报价就把这个杠杆废掉，因为买家会用你的小时价比价。 「现在窗口」三条论据，哪些站得住 工具质量门槛越过（4K / 一致性 / 产品形状稳定）在 2026 Q2 中小买家预期还锚在「AI=一眼假」，demo 转化率高 能交付商业级质量的供给端很薄，定价权在卖家 前两条是真的，但都是有时效性的： (1) 半年到一年内各家模型收敛后会摊平 (2) demo 包在网络里跑几轮，买家教育就完成 (3) 是唯一能撑住长尾利润的，但只保护首批建立口碑的接单者 实操含义：模型在未来 12 个月里回报最高。过完这个窗口，平台会有供应曲线追上来的同行打包同款服务、降到当前一半价格但质量近似。 案例没说的几件事 税费和平台抽成：Upwork 抽 10-20%。Stripe + 平台抽成是真实的成本。 退款 / 争议率：AI 视频在客户品味这一关的失败率不是零。 复购：作者提到转介绍，没披露 30K 月里「老客复购」vs「一次性单」的占比。 「30K 稳态」是带设计背景的接单者。纯 prompt 操作者没有审美经验，三个月后单价大概会落在 30K 的 50-70%。 结论 设计背景加产品视频做副业：案例内部自洽、数字合理、

title: Seedance 2.0 Product Video Case: From 8000 RMB Detail-Page Designer to 30K/Month AI Video Freelancer
description: Cold-eyed breakdown of an AI product-video freelance case sourced from the web. Backend math, international pricing tier, and the four-window argument for why "now" is the early period.
excerpt: A Hangzhou product designer switches from detail-page design to AI-generated product videos on Seeda
body: A Hangzhou product designer switches from detail-page design to AI-generated product videos on Seedance 2.0 in January 2026. Monthly pay goes from 8K RMB to about 30K RMB within six months. The post also quotes three international pricing tiers and a four-window argument for why the timing matters. The math, plus what is overstated. The six-month revenue curve, as posted Month Revenue (RMB) Notes 1 2,000 ten small gigs 2 5,000 referrals from earlier clients 3 10,000 one brand ordered a 20-video batch 6 30,000 "stable" (self-described) The curve reads as the front half of a typical freelance hockey stick. Months 1-2 are loss-leader pricing (the first gig was 200 RMB). From month 3 onwards volume matters more than rate. Month 6 plus is where referrals compound. 30K is steady-state, not peak. Treat it as the answer to "what does the plateau look like at this gig density," not "what can you earn at the top." Numbers are self-reported. The post does not post invoices. The cost structure collapse The interesting claim is not "AI video is good enough now." It is the cost-structure rewrite: This is the part that makes the case durable. title: Selling GPT Image services on Fiverr at $10–100 per gig: per-gig unit economics, deliverable disputes, and the platform churn curve
description: Cold read of an Fiverr-side-hustle case using GPT Image (and comparable models) for old-photo restoration, object removal, and AI product imagery — priced at $10–100 per gig. Below is per-gig unit economics, dispute / revision rates that compress margin, and the platform churn curve that decides which sellers actually keep the income.
excerpt: Fiverr gigs running on GPT Image (old-photo restoration at $10–25, object removal at $5–25, AI comme
body: Fiverr gigs running on GPT Image (old-photo restoration at $10–25, object removal at $5–25, AI commercial imagery at $20–100+) are a real category with thousands of active sellers. The per-gig math is clean: tool cost in cents, gig price in dollars, and a focused operator can ship 10–30 gigs per day. The margin compresses once dispute / revision rates, platform fees, and the Fiverr churn curve are factored in. Below is the unit economics, the three margin compressors, and the durability question. What the case lays out Category Gig price Tool cost Operator time Skill required Old-photo restoration $10–25 $0.05–0.30 5–15 min Mid Object removal $5–25 $0.05–0.20 3–10 min Low AI commercial imagery $20–100+ $0.20–0.80 10–25 min Mid–High The case frames these as three categories with similar economics. The differences in skill requirement and price ceiling matter more than the table implies — high-skill gigs (commercial imagery) carry lower volume but higher per-gig margin. Per-gig unit economics A focused operator shipping 15 gigs/day at an average $25 price generates roughly $300/day gross, or $7,500/month gross. title: Selling sales leads at $1K+/month: AI re-did the broker business
description: An X post (259 likes, 101 replies, 90 reposts) describes an information-arbitrage business: AI finds customers for other people (qualified leads), zero inventory, zero delivery. The product isn't the relationship — it's the structured data. The mechanism, unit economics, risk map, and four gaps the case does not address.
excerpt: An X post (259 likes, 101 replies, 90 reposts) describes an information-arbitrage business: use AI t
body: An X post (259 likes, 101 replies, 90 reposts) describes an information-arbitrage business: use AI to find customers for other people. Low ticket per pack, but 80%+ margin, zero inventory, zero delivery, and it can compound. The case is not 'broker upgraded.' It is: AI rebuilt the traditional broker business. What is sold is not the relationship — what is sold is the structured data: who is currently looking for your kind of service, who the decision-maker is, what the email is, what they've been doing lately. The mechanism, the unit economics, the risk map, and four gaps the case does not address, below. The mechanism, three steps The buyer's cold-outreach conversion is 5–10x higher than unfiltered cold call, because the signals are pre-filtered and the decision-maker is pre-located. Why this is "information arbitrage" What is sold is not the data itself — LinkedIn and company sites are public. What is sold is the processed product: 'filtered + ranked + timed.' A salesperson could scrape on their own, but one salesperson's weekly hours get them 50 leads of random quality. AI does 200/day filtered by signals like 'expanded in the last 7 days,' 'hiring a sales role,' 'just switched

title: Spott rebuilt Bullhorn in 6 months and hit 1M USD/month: why the boring old-software replacement is the best 2026 AI play
description: Spott — 3 founders from McKinsey / BCG / Bain, 2024 launch, 8x revenue growth in 6 months to 1M USD/month run rate (12M USD annualized). YC W25, 3.2M USD seed. The product replaces Bullhorn / Loxo / Vincere — the 20-year-old ATS / CRM stack used by recruiting agencies. The interesting part is not 'AI rebuilds recruiting software' — it is that the boring old-software replacement is the highest-payoff AI play. The unit economics, the migration moat, and the four gaps the case does not cover, below.
excerpt: Spott — 3 founders from McKinsey / BCG / Bain, 2024 launch, 8x revenue growth in 6 months to 1M USD/
body: Spott — 3 founders from McKinsey / BCG / Bain, 2024 launch, 8x revenue growth in 6 months to 1M USD/month (12M USD annualized). YC W25, 3.2M USD seed (Base10). The product replaces Bullhorn, Loxo, Vincere — the 20-year-old ATS / CRM stack used by recruiting agencies. The interesting part is not 'AI rebuilds recruiting software' — it is that the boring old-software replacement is the highest-payoff AI play. Demand is validated, pain is acute, migration barrier locks the win. The unit economics, the migration moat, and the four gaps the case does not cover, below. Why boring old-software replacement is the best AI play The 3 reasons compound. A reader scanning AI plays should weight boring old-software replacement over greenfield AI products because the demand side is solved. The risk is execution, not market. The unit economics at 1M USD/month The pricing math jumps 10–50x compared to incumbent ATS seats. The reason is the anchor — Spott sells against recruiter loaded cost (100K+ USD/year), not against software seat cost (1.2K–3.6K USD/year). title: The "30-day AI product bootcamp" pitch: a unit-economics teardown
description: A line-by-line read of a typical "30-day AI product monetization bootcamp" sales post, what it promises, what it leaves out, and how to decide whether the offer is worth the asking price.
excerpt: The post in question is a sales post for a 30-day AI product bootcamp. The pitch is: from product se
body: The post in question is a sales post for a 30-day AI product bootcamp. The pitch is: from product selection through build to promotion, in 30 days, taught in a cohort format, with a Quark-pan link to the curriculum. The format is familiar to most; this teardown applies to offers of this shape. The question is not whether the curriculum is real. It is whether the outcome the post implies (a "sustainable side-hustle" from a 30-day commitment) is what the curriculum actually delivers. What the pitch says, mapped to deliverables Reading the post literally, the offer promises four components: Product selection. Pick an AI-automation product category where the buyer has clear pain and budget. Build. Use no-code + AI to ship a v0 within 30 days. Promotion. Paid and organic distribution plan with templates. Long-term asset framing. "构建长期副业资产" (build a long-term side-hustle asset). The 30-day window is enough to ship (1) and (2). It is not enough to validate (3) or to demonstrate (4). What 30 days can and cannot do Stage 30-day outcome What it does not cover Selection Pick a category with margin 50% and clear buyer profile Validate that the chosen category has reachable distribution channel

title: The 17M USD a year an AI photo app kept by switching its payment rail off the App Store
description: A small AI photo-and-video team reportedly grew 2025 revenue 14x to 55M USD annualized, then to 70M USD. Same users, same product, different checkout. The 30% Apple cut is the lever most operators forget to pull.
excerpt: A small team running a consumer AI photo-and-video app reportedly grew revenue 14x in 2025, hit 55M
body: A small team running a consumer AI photo-and-video app reportedly grew revenue 14x in 2025, hit 55M USD annualized, and is now at 70M USD annualized. The product did not get a major model upgrade in that window. The growth came mostly from one operational decision: most of the paid funnel now runs through a self-hosted web checkout instead of the App Store. Below is what the case puts on the table and what it does not. The numbers Quantity Value 2025 revenue growth 14x year-over-year Annualized revenue (2025) 55M USD Annualized revenue (early 2026) 70M USD App Store category rank Top 5 in Photo & Video Funding None (bootstrapped) Styles (preset templates) 1,000 Cumulative renders 1 billion The figures are company-attributed. Not audited. They are useful as a directional argument, not as a financial statement. The payment-rail math The case lays out the cut in concrete numbers: That is the headline number. The case is explicit: same users, same product, different checkout. The 17M USD is more than most AI consumer teams spend on model API costs in a quarter. A second, quieter lever sits next to the cut: tax and refund handling. On the App Store, Apple handles VAT collection, sales-t

title: The Cross-border Pricing Formula (with Worked Examples)
description: The exact formula cross-border sellers use to price products on Shopify, Etsy and Amazon. Includes worked examples for dropshipping, branded products and high-AOV niches.
excerpt: Most sellers price by gut feel: cost × 3 sounds right. That works until the third month when the boo
body: Most sellers price by gut feel: cost × 3 sounds right. That works until the third month when the books show a loss. Here's the formula professional cross-border sellers actually use. The formula Where: cost = supplier price in USD shipping = per-order shipping cost to customer fixed fees = per-order platform fees (Shopify $0.30, PayPal $0.49 in some markets) commission + payment = combined percentage fee target margin = net profit % you want to keep Worked example 1 — branded Shopify product Cost: $8 Shipping: $3.50 Shopify Payments fee: 2.9% + $0.30 Target margin: 35% Profit per unit: $19.00 × (1 − 0.029) − 11.80 = $6.65 Worked example 2 — Etsy handmade Cost: $12 (materials + labor) Shipping: $5 (with $1 packaging) Etsy transaction fee: 6.5% Etsy payment processing: 3% + $0.25 Target margin: 40% Worked example 3 — Amazon FBA Cost: $6 Amazon referral fee: 15% FBA fulfillment: $4.50 Target margin: 25% The break-even price (0% margin) is $13.13. Anything above that is profit, anything below is a loss. Why markup-based pricing fails A 3× markup on $8 gives $24. But: Item Amount Revenue $24.00 Shopify fee (2.9% + $0.30) −$1.00 Payment processing (already included above) — Shipping −$3.

body: "Dropshipping is free to start" is the lie. Here's what a real store actually pays. The complete cost stack A store selling a $50 product via AliExpress dropshipping: Cost Amount % of revenue Revenue $50.00 100% Product cost $12.00 24% Shipping to customer $4.50 9% Shopify Basic $1.30 (allocated) 2.6% Shopify Payments $1.75 (2.9% + $0.30) 3.5% Apps (5 × $20/mo allocated) $3.30 6.6% Email tool $0.50 1% Subtotal before ads $26.65 53.3% Gross margin $23.35 46.7% Ad spend (Google + Meta) $10.00 20% Net margin before returns $13.35 26.7% Returns (8% of orders × $50 × cost basis 30%) $1.20 2.4% Chargebacks (1% × $15 fee) $0.15 0.3% Refund processing (refund fees kept by Stripe) $0.20 0.4% Customer service time (allocated) $0.50 1% Real net margin $11.30 22.6% That 22.6% looks fine. Now add the things most beginners forget: Hidden costs that erode profit Slow shipping refunds US customers expect delivery in 5–7 days. AliExpress shipping takes 15–25 days. 5% of customers request partial refunds ("where is my order?") or full refunds. Average payout on a slow-shipping complaint: $10 per affected customer. title: The Math of a 5K USD/Month Solo AI SaaS
description: How many paying users a one-person AI SaaS actually needs to clear 5K USD/mo. The LTV/CAC math behind three typical price points.
excerpt: A solo AI SaaS that replaces a salary needs to clear roughly 5K USD/month MRR. The number of paying
body: A solo AI SaaS that replaces a salary needs to clear roughly 5K USD/month MRR. The number of paying users that requires depends on three things: monthly price, customer acquisition cost, average customer lifetime. Plug in real numbers and the conclusion is uncomfortable: at typical solo-founder price points, you need many more users than the founding story suggests. The setup A solo founder, no employees, no sales team. Distribution through content, community, and one-time launches (Product Hunt, X, indie communities). No paid ads. A product that is genuinely useful — churn is the natural rate for the category, not artificially inflated by bad UX. Plug into the break-even calculator: Price CAC Avg lifetime Payback users Monthly MRR at payback 9 USD/mo 40 USD 6 months 27 243 USD 29 USD/mo 80 USD 8 months 23 667 USD 99 USD/mo 200 USD 12 months 25 2,475 USD Payback user count is roughly the same across price points (20-30 users) — the math is forgiving at the lower end. The painful number is the MRR at the payback point, which is price multiplied by user count. Hitting 5K USD MRR requires scaling 8-20x past break-even. title: TikTok Shop 佣金拆解：创作者和卖家到底付多少
description: TikTok Shop 2026 年的每一笔费用：推荐佣金、交易服务费、创作者分成、运费补贴规则、90 天退货窗口。$30 产品完整算账。
excerpt: TikTok Shop 的费率结构是主流电商平台里最短的，但惩罚那些不把创作者分成算进去的卖家。下面是一款 $30 产品的真实成本。 $30 产品的费用堆栈 费用 金额 占售价比 售价 $30.00
body: TikTok Shop 的费率结构是主流电商平台里最短的，但惩罚那些不把创作者分成算进去的卖家。下面是一款 $30 产品的真实成本。 $30 产品的费用堆栈 费用 金额 占售价比 售价 $30.00 100% 推荐佣金（6%，服饰类） $1.80 6% 交易服务费（1.8%） $0.54 1.8% TikTok 抽成 $2.34 7.8% 创作者分成（10% 常见） $3.00 10% TikTok 从创作者分成抽的 30% $0.90 3% 创作者 + TikTok 分成层 $3.90 13% 平台 + 创作者合计 $6.24 20.8% 这是底线。叠上 5% 退货率、10% 站外广告、$0.30 固定交易成本，画面就变了。 各品类推荐佣金 品类 推荐费 % 服饰（衣服、配件） 6% 家居 6% 电子 4% 美妆 5% 宠物用品 5% 玩具与收藏 5% 图书与媒体 4% 食品饮料 4% 汽车配件 4% 比 Amazon 15% 推荐费低得多。但创作者分成才是算账不严时的暴雷点。 创作者分成——隐形成本 大多数 TikTok Shop 卖家依赖创作者。卖家自己设佣金率（5–20% 常见），任何带你这件货的创作者都按这个比例拿销售分成。 创作者带一单时： 创作者得：佣金率 × 售价（如 10% × $30 = $3.00） TikTok 得：创作者所得的 30%（$3.00 × 30% = $0.90） 10% 的创作者分成实际成本是营收的 13%，不是 10%。 思路：把分成当营销成本算 把创作者分成当营销支出，不当平台费。 $30 产品、$9 成本、10% 创作者分成、8% 退货率、15% 广告费： 仍然健康，前提是创作者转化率 4% 以上。低于这个，60%+ 毛利会被分成层吃掉变成个位数。 退货窗口与成本 TikTok Shop 有 90 天退货窗口。大部分品类要求卖家付退货运费： 标准运费标签：$3.00–$4.50 超大件：$6.00–$9.00 $30 产品 10% 退货率，$4 退货运费，每卖一件平均被吃 $1.20，还没算仓储与重新上架。 新卖家激励 TikTok Shop 跑 90 天新卖家计划： 90 天 0% 推荐佣金（品类不同） 30 天 0% 交易服务费 最多 $1,500 创作者撮合 $200 广告金 相当于第一个月平台抽成为 0%，第二三个月降到 1.8%，然后跳回 6%+1.8%。上线量和促销后定价要按这个规划。 FBT（TikTok 发货）vs 卖家自发货 FBT 卖家自发货 履约费 $2.50–$4.50/件（按尺寸） 视情况 运费补贴 无要求 卖家吸收或转嫁 退货处理 $1.50/件 $3.00+/件 仓储 前 100 件免费，超出 $0.40/立方英尺/月 无 2 日达标志 有 无 FBT 提升算法排名，但加 $2.50

Add a 5% return rate, 10% offsite ad spend, and a $0.30 fixed transaction cost, and the picture changes.
